The two approaches are evaluated on open datasets comprising over 60,000 fundus and non-fundus images across multiple resolutions. Both approaches achieved an AUROC of 1.000 and accuracies between 0.999 and 1.000 on internal and external validation sets, showing comparable detection performance. The ML approach, however, exhibited substantially lower end-to-end latency while maintaining equivalent accuracy, indicating greater computational efficiency. These results suggest that for OOD detection tasks of limited visual complexity, lightweight ML approaches can achieve DL-level performance with significantly reduced computational cost, supporting practical real-world deployment.

</details>


### [252] [Developing a foundation model for high-resolution remote sensing data of the Netherlands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10184)
*Paul Vermeeren,Heysem Kaya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A foundation model trained on 1.2M satellite images of the Netherlands using a CNN-ViT hybrid, leveraging temporal data to achieve competitive global performance with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a generalizable remote sensing foundation model that can learn rich representations from limited, country-specific data while achieving competitive global performance.

Method: Combines CNN (for low-frequency features like textures/edges) and Vision Transformer (for high-frequency features like terrain structures). Uses temporal data as input to exploit time-dependent patterns, reducing feature ambiguity and improving generalization.

Result: Outperforms single-timepoint models on Netherlands vegetation monitoring. Achieves competitive results on global benchmarks despite using a smaller model and less pretraining data limited to the Netherlands.

Conclusion: The model demonstrates that rich, generalizable representations can be learned from limited geographic data, rivaling larger state-of-the-art models with far fewer parameters. Code and model are publicly released.

Abstract: We develop a foundation model using 1.2m high resolution satellite images of the Netherlands. By combining a Convolutional Neural Network and a Vision Transformer, the model captures both low- and high-frequency landscape features, such as fine textures, edges, and small objects as well as large terrain structures, elevation patterns, and land-cover distributions. Leveraging temporal data as input, the model learns from broader contextual information across time, allowing the model to exploit the temporal dependencies, such as topographic features, land-cover changes, and seasonal dynamics. These additional constraints reduce feature ambiguity, improve representation learning, and enable better generalization with fewer labeled samples. The foundation model is evaluated on multiple downstream tasks, ranging from use cases within the Netherlands to global benchmarking datasets. On the vegetation monitoring dataset of the Netherlands, the model shows clear performance improvements by incorporating temporal information instead of relying on a single time point. Despite using a smaller model and less pretraining data limited to the Netherlands, it achieves competitive results on global benchmarks when compared to state-of-the-art models. These results demonstrate that the model can learn rich, generalizable representations from limited data, achieving competitive performance on global benchmarks while using a fraction of the parameters of larger state-of-the-art remote sensing models. To maximize reproducibility and reuse, we made the scripts and the model accessible on GitHub.

</details>


### [253] [DynGhost: Temporally-Modelled Transformer for Dynamic Ghost Imaging with Quantum Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10185)
*Vittorio Palladino,Ahmet Enis Cetin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DynGhost is a transformer architecture for dynamic ghost imaging that uses alternating spatial/temporal attention and quantum-aware training (with Poisson noise models and Anscombe normalization) to significantly outperform existing methods, especially in photon-starved and dynamic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning ghost imaging methods fail on dynamic scenes because they can't exploit temporal coherence, and they use unrealistic additive Gaussian noise models instead of the true Poissonian statistics of real single-photon detectors (SNSPDs, SPADs, SiPMs).

Method: DynGhost uses a transformer with alternating spatial and temporal attention blocks, plus a quantum-aware training framework with physically accurate detector simulations and Anscombe variance-stabilizing normalization to handle Poisson noise.

Result: Outperforms both traditional reconstruction methods and existing deep learning architectures across multiple benchmarks, with particular gains in dynamic and photon-starved settings.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the two key limitations (temporal coherence and realistic noise modeling) for dynamic ghost imaging under real hardware constraints.

Abstract: Ghost imaging reconstructs spatial information from a single-pixel bucket detector by correlating structured illumination patterns with scalar intensity measurements. While deep learning approaches have achieved promising results on static scenes, two critical limitations remain unaddressed: existing architectures fail to exploit temporal coherence across frames, leaving dynamic ghost imaging largely unsolved, and they assume additive Gaussian noise models that do not reflect the true Poissonian statistics of real single-photon hardware. We present DynGhost (Dynamic Ghost Imaging Transformer), a transformer architecture that addresses both limitations through alternating spatial and temporal attention blocks. Our quantum-aware training framework, based on physically accurate detector simulations (SNSPDs, SPADs, SiPMs) and Anscombe variance-stabilizing normalization, resolves the distribution shift that causes classical models to fail under realistic hardware constraints. Experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that DynGhost outperforms both traditional reconstruction methods and existing deep learning architectures, with particular gains in dynamic and photon-starved settings.

</details>


### [254] [SciVQR: A Multidisciplinary Multimodal Benchmark for Advanced Scientific Reasoning Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10187)
*Longteng Guo,Xuanxu Lin,Dongze Hao,Tongtian Yue,Pengkang Huo,Jiatong Ma,Yuchen Liu,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SciVQR is a new multimodal benchmark spanning 54 scientific subfields that evaluates MLLMs on complex reasoning tasks combining visual comprehension with multi-step inference, revealing significant limitations in current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM benchmarks fail to capture the complexity and traceability of scientific reasoning processes needed for rigorous evaluation.

Method: Created a benchmark covering 54 subfields across 6 scientific disciplines, with domain-specific visuals (equations, charts, diagrams), tasks from factual recall to multi-step inference, and 46% of tasks including expert-authored solutions.

Result: Leading proprietary and open-source MLLMs show significant limitations in complex multimodal reasoning, particularly in multi-step inference and interdisciplinary knowledge integration.

Conclusion: There is a need for improved multi-step reasoning and interdisciplinary knowledge integration to advance MLLMs toward true scientific intelligence.

Abstract: Scientific reasoning is a key aspect of human intelligence, requiring the integration of multimodal inputs, domain expertise, and multi-step inference across various subjects. Existing benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail to capture the complexity and traceability of reasoning processes necessary for rigorous evaluation. To fill this gap, we introduce SciVQR, a multimodal benchmark covering 54 subfields in mathematics, physics, chemistry, geography, astronomy, and biology. SciVQR includes domain-specific visuals, such as equations, charts, and diagrams, and challenges models to combine visual comprehension with reasoning. The tasks range from basic factual recall to complex, multi-step inferences, with 46% including expert-authored solutions. SciVQR not only evaluates final answers but also examines the reasoning process, providing insights into how models reach their conclusions. Our evaluation of leading MLLMs, including both proprietary and open-source models, reveals significant limitations in handling complex multimodal reasoning tasks, underscoring the need for improved multi-step reasoning and better integration of interdisciplinary knowledge in advancing MLLMs toward true scientific intelligence. The dataset and evaluation code are publicly available at https://github.com/CASIA-IVA-Lab/SciVQR.

</details>


### [255] [DetRefiner: Model-Agnostic Detection Refinement with Feature Fusion Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10190)
*Soichiro Okazaki,Tatsuya Sasaki,Hiroki Ohashi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DetRefiner is a plug-and-play framework that fuses global and local features from foundational vision models (e.g., DINOv3) via a lightweight Transformer encoder to recalibrate base OVOD model confidence scores, achieving up to +10.1 AP on novel categories without requiring access to the base model's internal features or retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OVOD methods struggle to generalize to novel objects because they fail to adequately integrate global and local contextual cues.

Method: A lightweight Transformer encoder processes global image features and patch-level features from DINOv3 to produce a class vector and patch vectors. Attribute reliability is inferred to recalibrate the base model's confidence. The framework is plug-and-play, trained independently without accessing base model internals.

Result: Consistent improvements across COCO, LVIS, ODinW13, and Pascal VOC, with gains up to +10.1 AP on novel categories.

Conclusion: Learning to fuse global and local representations provides a powerful and general mechanism for advancing open-world object detection.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary object detection (OVOD) aims to detect both seen and unseen categories, yet existing methods often struggle to generalize to novel objects due to limited integration of global and local contextual cues. We propose DetRefiner, a simple yet effective plug-and-play framework that learns to fuse global and local features to refine open-vocabulary detection. DetRefiner processes global image features and patch-level image features from foundational models (e.g., DINOv3) through a lightweight Transformer encoder. The encoder produces a class vector capturing image-level attributes and patch vectors representing local region attributes, from which attribute reliability is inferred to recalibrate the base model's confidence. Notably, DetRefiner is trained independently of the base OVOD model, requiring neither access to its internal features nor retraining. At inference, it operates solely on the base detector's predictions, producing auxiliary calibration scores that are merged with the base detector's scores to yield the final refined confidence. Despite this simplicity, DetRefiner consistently enhances multiple OVOD models across COCO, LVIS, ODinW13, and Pascal VOC, achieving gains of up to +10.1 AP on novel categories. These results highlight that learning to fuse global and local representations offers a powerful and general mechanism for advancing open-world object detection. Our codes and models are available at https://github.com/hitachi-rd-cv/detrefiner.

</details>


### [256] [3DReflecNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for 3D Reconstruction of Reflective, Transparent, and Low-Texture Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10204)
*Zhicheng Liang,Haoyi Yu,Boyan Li,Dayou Zhang,Zijian Cao,Tianyi Gong,Junhua Liu,Shuguang Cui,Fangxin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3DReflecNet is a large-scale hybrid dataset (>22 TB, 7M+ frames) for benchmarking 3D reconstruction on challenging materials (reflective, transparent, low-texture), combining 120K+ synthetic and 1K+ real objects with benchmarks for 5 core tasks, showing SOTA methods struggle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets focus on diffuse, textured objects and provide limited insight into performance under real-world material complexities (reflective, transparent, low-texture surfaces) that violate key multi-view reconstruction assumptions.

Method: Constructed 3DReflecNet dataset with >120K synthetic instances (physically-based rendering of 12K+ shapes) and >1K real objects captured via consumer devices (>7M multi-view frames). Designed benchmarks for image matching, SfM, novel view synthesis, reflection removal, and relighting.

Result: State-of-the-art methods struggle to maintain accuracy across these settings, highlighting the need for more resilient 3D vision models.

Conclusion: 3DReflecNet exposes critical gaps in existing methods for challenging materials and provides a benchmark to drive development of more robust 3D vision models.

Abstract: Accurate 3D reconstruction of objects with reflective, transparent, or low-texture surfaces still remains notoriously challenging. Such materials often violate key assumptions in multi-view reconstruction pipelines, such as photometric consistency and the availability on distinct geometric texture cues. Existing datasets primarily focus on diffuse, textured objects, and therefore provide limited insight into performance under real-world material complexities. We introduce 3DReflecNet, a large-scale hybrid dataset exceeding 22 TB that is specifically designed to benchmark and advance 3D vision methods for these challenging materials. 3DReflecNet combines two types of data: over 120,000 synthetic instances generated via physically-based rendering of more than 12,000 shapes, and over 1,000 real-world objects captured using consumer devices. Together, these data consist of more than 7 million multi-view frames. The dataset spans diverse materials, complex lighting conditions, and a wide range of geometric forms, including shapes generated from both real and LLM-synthesized 2D images using diffusion-based pipelines. To support robust evaluation, we design benchmarks for five core tasks: image matching, structure-from-motion, novel view synthesis, reflection removal, and relighting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that state-of-the-art methods struggle to maintain accuracy across these settings, highlighting the need for more resilient 3D vision models.

</details>


### [257] [VPD-100K: Towards Generalizable and Fine-grained Visual Privacy Protection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10229)
*Xiaobin Hu,Enpu Zuo,Lanping Hu,Kaiwen Yang,Dianshu Liao,Tianyi Zhang,Bo Yin,Yinsi Zhou,Shidong Pan,Xiaoyu Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces VPD-100K, a large-scale fine-grained visual privacy dataset with 100K images, 33 fine-grained classes, and 190K+ instances across four privacy domains, along with a frequency-enhanced lightweight detection module that outperforms existing methods on image and video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing privacy detection models are hindered by the lack of comprehensive datasets—current privacy-oriented datasets suffer from limited scale, coarse-grained annotations, and narrow domain coverage, failing to capture real-world sensitive information details.

Method: The authors constructed VPD-100K (100K images, 33 fine-grained classes, 190K+ instances across Human Presence, On-Screen PII, Physical Identifiers, and Location Indicators), and designed a frequency-enhanced lightweight module with frequency-domain attention fusion and adaptive spectral gating mechanism.

Result: Extensive experiments on diverse image and streaming video benchmarks show the dataset and frequency mechanism effectively improve privacy detection performance, especially for subtle details in unconstrained scenarios like live streaming.

Conclusion: VPD-100K addresses the critical data gap in privacy detection with large-scale fine-grained annotations, and the frequency-enhanced module effectively captures subtle sensitive information beyond spatial pixel limitations.

Abstract: Privacy protection has become a critical requirement in the era of ubiquitous visual data sharing, imposing higher demands on efficient and robust privacy detection algorithms. However, current robust detection models are severely hindered by the lack of comprehensive datasets. Existing privacy-oriented datasets often suffer from limited scale, coarse-grained annotations, and narrow domain coverage, failing to capture the intricate details of sensitive information in realworld environments. To bridge this gap, we present a large-scale, fine-grained Visual Privacy Dataset (VPD-100K), designed to facilitate generalized privacy detection. We establish a holistic taxonomy comprising four primary domains: Human Presence, On-Screen Personally Identifiable Information (PII), Physical Identifiers, and Location Indicators, containing 100,000 images annotated with 33 fine-grained classes and over 190,000 object instances. Statistical analysis reveals that our dataset features long-tailed distributions, small object scales, and high visual complexity. These characteristics make the dataset particularly valuable for demanding, unconstrained applications such as live streaming, where actors frequently face unintentional, realtime information leakage. Furthermore, we design an effective frequency-enhanced lightweight module consisting of frequency-domain attention fusion and adaptive spectral gating mechanism that breaks the limitations of spatial pixel intensity to better capture the subtle details of sensitive information. Extensive experiments conducted on both diverse image and streaming videos benchmarks consistently demonstrate the effectiveness of our VPD-100K dataset and the wellcurated frequency mechanism. The code and dataset are available at https://vpd-100k.github.io/.

</details>


### [258] [AdaptSplat: Adapting Vision Foundation Models for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10239)
*Mingwei Xing,Xinliang Wang,Yifeng Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdaptSplat introduces a lightweight 1.5M-parameter Frequency-Preserving Adapter (FPA) for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting that extracts direction-aware high-frequency structural priors from shallow features of a vision foundation model, compensating for high-frequency attenuation in deep features and achieving SOTA cross-domain generalization and geometric fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward 3DGS methods suffer from (1) scale bottleneck of 3D training data and (2) low-pass filtering effect of deep networks, leading to poor cross-domain generalization and low high-frequency geometric fidelity.

Method: A Frequency-Preserving Adapter (FPA) with only 1.5M parameters: extracts direction-aware high-frequency structural priors from shallow features of a vision foundation model, integrates them via high-frequency positional encodings and adaptive residual modulation into the generic pipeline (feature extraction → multi-view interaction → feature decoding).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction performance on multiple standard benchmarks with stable cross-domain generalization.

Conclusion: A simple single adapter design can outperform complex architecture-specific designs for feed-forward 3DGS, proving that addressing high-frequency attenuation is key to geometric fidelity and generalization.

Abstract: This work explores a simple yet powerful lightweight adapter design for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Existing methods typically apply complex, architecture-specific designs on top of the generic pipeline of image feature extraction $\rightarrow$ multi-view interaction $\rightarrow$ feature decoding. However, constrained by the scale bottleneck of 3D training data and the low-pass filtering effect of deep networks, these methods still fall short in cross-domain generalization and high-frequency geometric fidelity. To address these problems, we propose AdaptSplat, which demonstrates that without complex component engineering, introducing a single adapter of only 1.5M parameters into the generic architecture is sufficient to achieve superior performance. Specifically, we design a lightweight Frequency-Preserving Adapter (FPA) that extracts direction-aware high-frequency structural priors from the shallow features of a powerful vision foundation model backbone, and seamlessly integrates them into the generic pipeline via high-frequency positional encodings and adaptive residual modulation. This effectively compensates for the high-frequency attenuation caused by over-smoothing in deep features, improving the fitting accuracy of Gaussian primitives on complex surfaces and sharp boundaries. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AdaptSplat achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction performance on multiple standard benchmarks, with stable generalization across domains. Code available at: https://github.com/xmw666/AdaptSplat.

</details>


### [259] [Efficient Hybrid CNN-GNN Architecture for Monocular Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10251)
*Ishan Narayan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GraphDepth integrates Graph Neural Networks (GraphSAGE) into a U-Net backbone for monocular depth estimation, achieving competitive accuracy with lower computational cost than transformer-based methods by modeling long-range spatial relationships via linear-complexity graph message passing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard convolutional depth estimation methods have limited receptive fields and cannot capture long-range spatial relationships. Transformer-based alternatives offer global context but suffer from quadratic complexity. GraphDepth aims to achieve global reasoning with linear scalability.

Method: (1) Batch-parallelized graph construction with k-NN/grid-based adjacency; (2) Multi-scale GraphSAGE layers at bottleneck and decoder stages (1/32, 1/16, 1/8 resolution); (3) Channel-attention gated skip connections; (4) Heteroscedastic uncertainty estimation head with aleatoric uncertainty.

Result: Within 4.6% of SOTA transformers on NYU Depth V2; best result on WHU Aerial (RMSE 8.24 m); superior zero-shot transfer to Mid-Air; 25 FPS vs 9 FPS, 3.8 GB vs 8.8 GB VRAM compared to transformers.

Conclusion: Graph-based relational reasoning via GNNs is an efficient alternative to transformers for monocular depth estimation, offering strong generalization and competitive accuracy at lower computational cost.

Abstract: We present GraphDepth, a monocular depth estimation architecture that synergistically integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) within a convolutional encoder-decoder framework. Our approach embeds efficient GraphSAGE layers at multiple scales of a ResNet-101 U-Net backbone, enabling explicit modeling of long-range spatial relationships that lie beyond the receptive field of local convolutions. Key technical contributions include: (1) batch-parallelized graph construction with configurable k-NN and
  grid-based adjacency for scalable training; (2) multi-scale GraphSAGE integration at bottleneck and decoder stages
  (1/32, 1/16, 1/8 resolution) to propagate global context
  throughout the feature hierarchy; (3) channel-attention gated skip connections that adaptively weight encoder
  features before fusion; and (4) heteroscedastic uncertainty estimation via a dedicated aleatoric
  uncertainty head, enabling confidence-aware loss weighting during
  optimization. Unlike transformer-based hybrids, which suffer from quadratic complexity in sequence length, GraphDepth scales linearly with spatial resolution while achieving comparable global receptive fields through iterative message passing. Experiments on NYU Depth V2, WHU Aerial, ETH3D, and Mid-Air benchmarks demonstrate competitive accuracy within 4.6\% of state-of-the-art transformers on indoor scenes with substantially lower computational cost (25 FPS vs 9 FPS, 3.8 GB vs 8.8 GB VRAM). GraphDepth achieves the best reported result on WHU Aerial (RMSE 8.24 m) and exhibits superior zero-shot cross-domain transfer to the Mid-Air synthetic aerial dataset, validating the generalization power of explicit relational reasoning for depth estimation.

</details>


### [260] [PolarVSR: A Unified Framework and Benchmark for Continuous Space-Time Polarization Video Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10275)
*Chenggong Li,Yidong Luo,Junchao Zhang,Boxin Shi,Degui Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first space-time polarization video reconstruction architecture that jointly models polarization directions in space and time using implicit neural representation for continuous upsampling, and introduces a flow-guided polarization variation loss. It also establishes the first large-scale color DoFP polarization video benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DoFP color polarization imaging faces challenges in recovering polarization parameters from mosaic arrays, hardware bottlenecks, and inability to support high-frame-rate acquisition, limiting its use in dynamic video tasks.

Method: A space-time polarization video reconstruction architecture that jointly models polarization directions in space and time, using polarization-aware implicit neural representation for continuous high-fidelity upsampling, with a flow-guided polarization variation loss to supervise polarization dynamics.

Result: Extensive experiments on the newly established large-scale color DoFP polarization video benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Conclusion: The proposed space-time reconstruction approach effectively addresses limitations in DoFP polarization imaging for dynamic video tasks, enabling joint spatial and temporal enhancement.

Abstract: Polarimetric imaging captures surface polarization characteristics, such as the Degree of Linear Polarization (DoLP) and the Angle of Polarization (AoP). In mainstream Division of-Focal-Plane (DoFP) color polarization imaging, recovering polarization parameters from captured mosaic arrays remains a challenging inverse problem. Existing DoFP cameras also face hardware bottlenecks and often cannot support high-frame-rate acquisition, limiting polarimetric imaging in dynamic video tasks. These limitations motivate joint spatial and temporal enhancement. To this end, we propose the first space-time polarization video reconstruction architecture. The method jointly models polarization directions in space and time and uses a polarization-aware implicit neural representation for continuous, high-fidelity upsampling. By analyzing temporal variations in polarization parameters, we further introduce a flow-guided polarization variation loss to supervise polarization dynamics. We also establish the first large-scale color DoFP polarization video benchmark to support this research direction. Extensive experiments on this benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the method.

</details>


### [261] [LimeCross: Context-Conditioned Layered Image Editing with Structural Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10319)
*Ryugo Morita,Stanislav Frolov,Brian Bernhard Moser,Ko Watanabe,Riku Takahashi,Issey Sukeda,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LimeCross is a training-free framework for context-conditioned layered image editing that edits individual RGBA layers based on text prompts while maintaining cross-layer consistency and layer integrity, outperforming existing methods on the newly introduced LayerEditBench benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual editing of layered images requires careful cross-layer coordination, while AI pipelines flatten then decompose layers, causing background-to-foreground leakage and unstable transparency.

Method: A training-free framework using bi-stream attention mechanism to leverage contextual cues from other layers while maintaining explicit layer integrity.

Result: LimeCross improves layer purity and composite realism over strong editing baselines on the LayerEditBench benchmark (1500 layered scenes).

Conclusion: Context-conditioned layered editing is established as a principled framework for controllable generative creation.

Abstract: Layered image assets are widely used in real-world creative workflows, enabling non-destructive iteration and flexible re-composition. Recent advances in layered image generation and decomposition synthesize or recover layered representations, yet controllable editing of layered images remains challenging. Manual editing requires careful coordination across layers to maintain consistent illumination and contact, while AI-based pipelines collapse layers into a flattened image for editing, then decompose them again, introducing background-to-foreground leakage and unstable transparency. To address these limitations, we propose LimeCross, a training-free context-conditioned layered image editing framework that edits user-selected RGBA layers according to text while keeping the remaining layers unchanged. It leverages contextual cues from other layers using a bi-stream attention mechanism to preserve cross-layer consistency, while explicitly maintaining layer integrity to prevent the contamination of edited layers. To evaluate our approach, we introduce LayerEditBench, a benchmark of 1500 layered scenes with paired source/target prompts, along with evaluation protocols that assess both edit fidelity and alpha channel stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LimeCross improves layer purity and composite realism over strong editing baselines, establishing context-conditioned layered editing as a principled framework for controllable generative creation.

</details>


### [262] [The Alpha Blending Hypothesis: Compositing Shortcut in Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10334)
*Andrii Yermakov,Jan Cech,Mario Fritz,Jiri Matas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deepfake detectors often work by detecting alpha blending artifacts rather than semantic anomalies; BlenD method using self-blended images achieves state-of-the-art cross-dataset generalization without training on generated deepfakes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deepfake detection methods show improved cross-dataset generalization, but the underlying mechanisms are not well understood.

Method: Introduces Alpha Blending Hypothesis; proposes BlenD which trains on large-scale real-only facial images augmented with self-blended images (SBI), avoiding explicitly generated deepfakes during training.

Result: Achieves best average cross-dataset generalization on 15 compositional deepfake datasets (2019-2025); ensemble with blending-resilient models yields 94.0% AUROC.

Conclusion: Frame-based deepfake detectors primarily function as alpha blending searchers; blending-artifact-based and blending-resilient models are complementary for improved detection.

Abstract: Recent deepfake detection methods demonstrate improved cross-dataset generalization, yet the underlying mechanisms remain underexplored. We introduce the Alpha Blending Hypothesis, positing that state-of-the-art frame-based detectors primarily function as alpha blending searchers; rather than learning semantic anomalies or specific generative neural fingerprints, they localize low-level compositing artifacts introduced during the integration of manipulated faces into target frames. We experimentally validate the hypothesis, demonstrating that deepfake detectors exhibit high sensitivity to the so-called self-blended images (SBI) and non-generative manipulations. We propose the method BlenD that leverages a large-scale, diverse dataset of real-only facial images augmented with SBI. This approach achieves the best average cross-dataset generalization on 15 compositional deepfake datasets released between 2019 and 2025 without utilizing explicitly generated deepfakes during training. Furthermore, we show that predictions from explicit blending searchers and models resilient to blending shortcuts are highly complementary, yielding a state-of-the-art AUROC of 94.0% in an ensemble configuration. The code with experiments and the trained model will be publicly released.

</details>


### [263] [EvoStreaming: Your Offline Video Model Is a Natively Streaming Assistant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10343)
*Zichen Wen,Boxue Yang,Junlong Ke,Jiajie Huang,Chenfei Liao,Junxi Wang,Xuyang Liu,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RealStreamEval evaluates video LLMs in streaming settings, and EvoStreaming adapts offline models to streaming with only 1,000 self-generated samples, improving scores by up to 10.8 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VideoLLMs are trained for offline inference, and streaming benchmarks externalize timing decisions to evaluators; there is a need for a protocol that penalizes unnecessary responses and adaptation methods for streaming.

Method: Proposes RealStreamEval (frame-level multi-turn evaluation protocol) and EvoStreaming (self-evolved streaming adaptation using the base model as data generator, relevance annotator, and rollout policy with only 1,000 self-generated samples).

Result: EvoStreaming improves RealStreamEval scores by up to 10.8 points across five VideoLLM backbones while largely preserving offline performance, using 139× fewer samples than prior streaming instruction-tuning.

Conclusion: Data-efficient interaction tuning is a practical path for adapting offline VideoLLMs to streaming assistants.

Abstract: Streaming video understanding demands more than watching longer videos: assistants must decide when to speak in real time, balancing responsiveness against verbosity. Yet most video-language models (VideoLLMs) are trained for offline inference, and existing streaming benchmarks externalize this timing decision to the evaluator. We address this gap with RealStreamEval, a frame-level multi-turn evaluation protocol that exposes models to sequential observations and penalizes unnecessary responses. Under this protocol, we observed that strong offline VideoLLMs retain useful visual understanding but lack an interaction policy for deciding when to respond. Motivated by this observation, we propose EvoStreaming, a self-evolved streaming adaptation framework in which the base model itself acts as data generator, relevance annotator, and roll-out policy to synthesize streaming trajectories without external supervision. With only $1{,}000$ self-generated samples ($139\times$ less than the leading streaming instruction-tuning approach) and no architectural changes, EvoStreaming consistently improves the overall RealStreamEval score by up to $10.8$ points across five open VideoLLM backbones (Qwen2/2.5/3-VL, InternVL-3.5, MiniCPM-V4.5) while largely preserving offline video performance. These results suggest that data-efficient interaction tuning is a practical path for adapting existing VideoLLMs to streaming assistants.

</details>


### [264] [BGG: Bridging the Geometric Gap between Cross-View images by Vision Foundation Model Adaptation for Geo-Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10345)
*Wei Wang,Dou Quan,Ning Huyan,Shuang Wang,Yi Li,Pei He,Licheng Jiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BGG is a parameter-efficient adaptation framework that uses a vision foundation model (DINOv3) with two modules (MFEA and FASA) to bridge geometric gaps in cross-view geo-localization, achieving state-of-the-art results on University-1652 and SUES-200 datasets with low training costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-view geo-localization is challenged by geometric differences between drone and satellite images, and existing methods struggle to bridge this gap efficiently.

Method: BGG leverages a vision foundation model (DINOv3) with two key modules: (1) Multi-granularity Feature Enhancement Adapter (MFEA) using multi-level dilated convolutions for scale/viewpoint robustness, and (2) Frequency-Aware Structural Aggregation (FASA) that modulates patch tokens in frequency domain for adaptive local feature enhancement, then fuses enhanced local features with the [CLS] token.

Result: Extensive experiments on University-1652 and SUES-200 datasets show BGG achieves state-of-the-art localization performance with significant advantages over other methods and low training costs.

Conclusion: BGG effectively bridges cross-view geometric gaps by adapting a vision foundation model with parameter-efficient modules, enabling superior geo-localization with minimal training overhead.

Abstract: Geometric differences between cross-view images, such as drone and satellite views, significantly increase the challenge of Cross-View Geo-Localization (CVGL), which aims to acquire the geolocation of images by image retrieval. To further enhance the CVGL performance, this paper proposes a parameter-efficient adaptation framework for bridging the geometric gap across images based on the vision foundation model (VFM) (e.g., DINOv3), termed BGG. BGG not only effectively leverages the general visual representations of VFM and captures the robust and consistent features from cross-view images, but also utilizes the generalization capabilities of the VFM, significantly improving the CVGL performance. It mainly contains a Multi-granularity Feature Enhancement Adapter (MFEA) and a Frequency-Aware Structural Aggregation (FASA) module. Specifically, MFEA enhances the scale adaptability and viewpoint robustness of features by multi-level dilated convolutions, effectively bridging the cross-view geometric gap with small training costs. Additionally, considering the [CLS] token lacks spatial details for precise image retrieval and localization, the FASA module modulates patch tokens in the frequency domain and performs adaptive aggregation for local structural feature enhancement. Finally, BGG fuses the enhanced local features with the [CLS] token for more accurate CVGL. Extensive experiments on University-1652 and SUES-200 datasets demonstrate that BGG has significant advantages over other methods and achieves state-of-the-art localization performance with low training costs.

</details>


### [265] [Portable Active Learning for Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10349)
*Rashi Sharma,Justin Timothy C. Bersamin,Karthikk Subramanian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PAL is a detector-agnostic active learning framework that uses only inference outputs (lightweight logistic classifiers + image-level diversity) to select informative bounding box annotations, improving label efficiency across multiple detection benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bounding box annotation is costly and limits scalability. Prior active learning methods depend on model internals or modify training pipelines, increasing integration overhead, and rarely jointly exploit image-level signals, class imbalance, and instance-level uncertainty.

Method: PAL operates solely on inference outputs: (1) trains lightweight class-specific logistic classifiers to distinguish true/false positives for entropy-based instance uncertainty; (2) refines candidate selection via global image entropy, class diversity, and image similarity to form informative & diverse batches.

Result: Consistent improvements in label efficiency and detection accuracy over existing baselines on COCO, PASCAL VOC, and BDD100K.

Conclusion: PAL is a practical, detector-agnostic active learning solution that reduces annotation cost without modifying detector internals, enabling scalable deployment of object detection in real-world settings.

Abstract: Annotating bounding boxes is costly and limits the scalability of object detection. This challenge is compounded by the need to preserve high accuracy while minimizing manual effort in real-world applications. Prior active learning methods often depend on model features or modify detector internals and training schedules, increasing integration overhead. Moreover, they rarely jointly exploit the benefits of image-level signals, class-imbalance cues, and instance-level uncertainty for comprehensive selection. We present Portable Active Learning (PAL), a detector-agnostic, easily portable framework that operates solely on inference outputs. PAL combines class-wise instance uncertainty with image-level diversity to guide data selection. At each round, PAL trains lightweight class-specific logistic classifiers to distinguish true from false positives, producing entropy-based uncertainty scores for proposals. Candidate images are then refined using global image entropy, class diversity, and image similarity, yielding batches that are both informative and diverse. PAL requires no changes to model internals or training pipelines, ensuring broad compatibility across detectors. Extensive experiments on COCO, PASCAL VOC, and BDD100K demonstrate that PAL consistently improves label efficiency and detection accuracy compared to existing active learning baselines, making it a practical solution for scalable and cost-effective deployment of object detection in real-world settings.

</details>


### [266] [DySurface: Consistent 4D Surface Reconstruction via Bridging Explicit Gaussians and Implicit Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10360)
*Minje Kim,Younghyun Noh,Jaesoon Kim,Tae-Kyun Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DySurface bridges 3DGS and implicit SDFs for dynamic scene surface reconstruction, using deformed Gaussians to guide an SDF field via a VoxGS-DSDF branch, achieving superior geometric accuracy with watertight surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NVS for dynamic scenes suffers from geometric ambiguities (discontinuous/surface artifacts) when relying solely on photometric optimization in NeRF/3DGS methods.

Method: Proposes DySurface with a VoxGS-DSDF branch that uses deformed Gaussians to construct a dynamic sparse voxel grid, providing explicit geometric guidance to an implicit SDF field, reconciling forward (canonical→dynamic) and backward (dynamic→canonical) deformations.

Result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in geometric accuracy metrics while maintaining competitive rendering performance.

Conclusion: Explicit anchoring from Gaussians effectively regularizes volumetric SDF rendering, producing watertight boundaries and detailed surfaces in dynamic scenes.

Abstract: While novel view synthesis (NVS) for dynamic scenes has seen significant progress, reconstructing temporally consistent geometric surfaces remains a challenge. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) offer powerful dynamic scene rendering capabilities; however, relying solely on photometric optimization often leads to geometric ambiguities. This results in discontinuous surfaces, severe artifacts, and broken surfaces over time. To address these limitations, we present DySurface, a novel framework that bridges the effectiveness of explicit Gaussians with the geometric fidelity of implicit Signed Distance Functions (SDFs) in dynamic scenes. Our approach tackles the structural discrepancy between the forward deformation of 3DGS ($canonical \rightarrow dynamic$) and the backward deformation required for volumetric SDF rendering ($dynamic \rightarrow canonical$). Specifically, we propose the VoxGS-DSDF branch that leverages deformed Gaussians to construct a dynamic sparse voxel grid, providing explicit geometric guidance to the implicit SDF field. This explicit anchoring effectively regularizes the volumetric rendering process, significantly improving surface reconstruction quality, with watertight boundaries and detailed representations. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that DySurface significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in geometric accuracy metrics while maintaining competitive rendering performance.

</details>


### [267] [CellDX AI Autopilot: Agent-Guided Training and Deployment of Pathology Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10362)
*Alexey Pchelnikov,Aleksei Pchelnikov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CellDX AI Autopilot is a platform that lets users train pathology AI models through natural language, using pre-processed data and an agent skill architecture to reduce tuning cost by 30x and eliminate the need for ML expertise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training computational pathology models currently requires expensive whole-slide-image datasets, GPU infrastructure, deep ML expertise, and substantial engineering effort, creating bottlenecks in both diagnostic pathology adoption and researcher productivity.

Method: The platform provides agent skills (dataset curation, hyperparameter tuning, model comparison, human-in-the-loop deployment), an MIL framework with four classification strategies, and iterative pairwise hyperparameter search (grid or seeded random) on a pre-built dataset of 32K cases/66K images with pre-extracted features.

Result: Iterative pairwise hyperparameter search reduces tuning cost by over 30x compared to exhaustive search. The system exposes pathology-specialized agent skills to general-purpose AI agents (any LLM-based runtime).

Conclusion: CellDX AI Autopilot is the first system to combine pathology-specialized agent skills with a general-purpose LLM agent runtime, enabling end-to-end automated model training without requiring domain-specific agents, addressing both the ML-expertise and engineering bottlenecks.

Abstract: Training AI models for computational pathology currently requires access to expensive whole-slide-image datasets, GPU infrastructure, deep expertise in machine learning, and substantial engineering effort. We present CellDX AI Autopilot, a platform that lets users -- from pathologists with no ML background to ML practitioners running many parallel experiments -- train, evaluate, and deploy whole-slide image classifiers through natural language interaction with an AI agent. The platform provides a structured set of agent skills that guide the user through dataset curation, automated hyperparameter tuning, multi-strategy model comparison, and human-in-the-loop deployment, all on a pre-built dataset of over 32,000 cases and 66,000 H&E-stained whole-slide images with pre-extracted features. We describe the agent skill architecture, the underlying Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) training framework supporting four classification strategies, and an iterative pairwise hyperparameter search (grid or seeded random) that reduces tuning cost by over 30x compared to exhaustive search. CellDX AI Autopilot is, to our knowledge, the first system to expose pathology-specialized agent skills and a pathology-specialized training platform to general-purpose AI agents (e.g. any LLM-based agent runtime), delivering end-to-end automated model training without requiring the agent itself to be domain-specific. The platform addresses both the ML-expertise bottleneck that limits adoption in diagnostic pathology and the engineering bottleneck that limits how many experiments a researcher can run cost-effectively.

</details>


### [268] [Halo Separation-guided Underwater Multi-scale Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10374)
*Jiaxin Yang,Honglin Liu,Yongli Wang,Shuyi Cao,Chengcheng Jiang,Jiale Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a single halo image correction method using an iterative structure with two sub-networks (halo separation and multi-scale recovery) to remove artificial light halos from underwater AUV images, trained on UIEB/EUVP datasets and tested on real underwater halo images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing underwater image enhancement methods fail to address halos caused by artificial light sources on AUVs, which seriously degrade image quality and affect subsequent vision tasks.

Method: A two-sub-network iterative structure: (1) halo layer separation sub-network using gradient minimization, (2) multi-scale recovery sub-network to recover masked information. Radial gradient constraints are introduced based on brightness distribution analysis.

Result: Trained on UIEB and EUVP synthetic datasets, tested on real underwater halo images captured with artificial light.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively corrects halo artifacts from artificial light sources, improving underwater image restoration quality for AUV applications.

Abstract: Underwater images captured by Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) are inevitably affected by artificial light sources, which often produce halos in the foreground of the camera and seriously interfere with the quality of the image. The existing underwater image enhancement methods fail to fully consider this key problem, and the robustness of processing images under artificial light scenes is poor. In practical applications, since underwater image enhancement itself is a very challenging task, the influence of artificial light sources will lead to serious degradation of image performance and affect subsequent vision tasks. In order to effectively deal with this problem, this paper designs a single halo image correction method based on an iterative structure. The network is mainly divided into two sub-networks, one is the halo layer separation sub-network which aims to separate the halo by gradient minimization, and the other is the multi-scale recovery sub-network which aims to recover the image information masked by halo. The UIEB and EUVP synthetic datasets are used for training to ensure that the network can fully learn the characteristics and laws of underwater halo images. Then a large number of halo images taken in an underwater environment with real artificial light are collected for testing. In addition, the brightness distribution characteristics of underwater halo images are analyzed and the radial gradient is introduced to constraint eliminate halo to improve the effect of underwater image restoration.

</details>


### [269] [SleepWalk: A Three-Tier Benchmark for Stress-Testing Instruction-Guided Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10376)
*Niyati Rawal,Sushant Ravva,Shah Alam Abir,Saksham Jain,Aman Chadha,Vinija Jain,Suranjana Trivedy,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SleepWalk is a benchmark that evaluates Vision-Language Models on their ability to predict spatially coherent, executable trajectories in 3D environments from natural-language instructions. Tests on 2,472 environments with 3 frontier VLMs reveal systematic failures in grounded spatial reasoning, especially under occlusion, interaction constraints, and multi-step instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs excel in multimodal perception but it's unclear if they can reliably ground language into spatially coherent, executable actions in 3D environments. Existing navigation benchmarks focus on long-range exploration across rooms rather than localized, interaction-centric embodied reasoning.

Method: SleepWalk generates single-scene 3D worlds from textual descriptions, filtered for navigability, and tasks models with predicting collision-free trajectories ending at action-compatible locations given visual observations and natural-language instructions. It uses a pointwise judge-based evaluation protocol across three difficulty tiers.

Result: Frontier VLMs show systematic failures in grounded spatial reasoning, with performance dropping as task difficulty increases, particularly under occlusion, interaction constraints, and multi-step instructions. Current VLMs can produce somewhat coherent and executable trajectories but fall short reliably.

Conclusion: SleepWalk provides a critical benchmark for advancing grounded multimodal reasoning, embodied planning, vision-language navigation, and action-capable agents, exposing current VLM limitations in a controlled yet scalable 3D setting.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have advanced rapidly in multimodal perception and language understanding, yet it remains unclear whether they can reliably ground language into spatially coherent, plausibly executable actions in 3D digital environments. We introduce SleepWalk, a benchmark for evaluating instruction-grounded trajectory prediction in single-scene 3D worlds generated from textual scene descriptions and filtered for navigability. Unlike prior navigation benchmarks centered on long-range exploration across rooms, SleepWalk targets localized, interaction-centric embodied reasoning: given rendered visual observations and a natural-language instruction, a model must predict a trajectory that respects scene geometry, avoids collisions, and terminates at an action-compatible location. The benchmark covers diverse indoor and outdoor environments and organizes tasks into three tiers of spatial and temporal difficulty, enabling fine-grained analysis of grounding under increasing compositional complexity. Using a standardized pointwise judge-based evaluation protocol, we evaluate three frontier VLMs on 2,472 curated 3D environments with nine instructions per scene. Results reveal systematic failures in grounded spatial reasoning, especially under occlusion, interaction constraints, and multi-step instructions: performance drops as the difficulty level of the tasks increase. In general, current VLMs can somewhat produce trajectories that are simultaneously spatially coherent, plausibly executable, and aligned with intended actions. By exposing failures in a controlled yet scalable setting, SleepWalk provides a critical benchmark for advancing grounded multimodal reasoning, embodied planning, vision-language navigation, and action-capable agents in 3D environments.

</details>


### [270] [Sens-VisualNews: A Benchmark Dataset for Sensational Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10394)
*Andreas Goulas,Damianos Galanopoulos,Evlampios Apostolidis,Vasileios Mezaris*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces the task of sensational image detection in news media, presents a new benchmark dataset (Sens-VisualNews) with 9,576 annotated images, and evaluates various Multimodal LLMs on this task in zero-shot and fine-tuned settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensational content triggers physiological arousal that bypasses critical evaluation and accelerates viral sharing, making its detection critical for identifying check-worthy content and flagging potential disinformation.

Method: The authors create Sens-VisualNews, a benchmark of 9,576 news images annotated for sensational concepts/events. They then evaluate open SotA Multimodal LLMs in zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, studying prompt sensitivity, performance, and robustness.

Result: The study provides a comprehensive evaluation of various open Multimodal LLMs on the new sensational image detection task, analyzing their performance and robustness across different settings.

Conclusion: The Sens-VisualNews dataset and benchmark enable further research on sensational image detection, which can serve as a critical filtering mechanism against disinformation.

Abstract: The detection of sensational content in media items can be a critical filtering mechanism for identifying check-worthy content and flagging potential disinformation, since such content triggers physiological arousal that often bypasses critical evaluation and accelerates viral sharing. In this paper we introduce the task of sensational image detection, which aims to determine whether an image contains shocking, provocative, or emotionally charged features to grab attention and trigger strong emotional responses. To support research on this task, we create a new benchmark dataset (called Sens-VisualNews) that contains 9,576 images from news items, annotated based on the (in-)existence of various sensational concepts and events in their visual content. Finally, using Sens-VisualNews, we study the prompt sensitivity, performance and robustness of a wide range of open SotA Multimodal LLMs, across both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings.

</details>


### [271] [AnomalyClaw: A Universal Visual Anomaly Detection Agent via Tool-Grounded Refutation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10397)
*Xi Jiang,Yinjie Zhao,Zesheng Yang,Feng Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnomalyClaw is a training-free VAD agent that uses multi-round refutation to improve VLM-based anomaly detection, achieving consistent gains (up to +7.93 pp macro-AUROC) on CrossDomainVAD-12 benchmark across multiple VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-domain VAD models struggle to transfer across domains due to varying anomaly definitions, data modalities, and annotation standards; single-inference VLMs are unreliable as they rely more on prior knowledge than on normal-sample references or fine-grained evidence.

Method: AnomalyClaw converts anomaly judgment into a multi-round refutation process: in each round, the agent proposes candidates and refutes them against normal-sample references using a 13-tool library (visual verification, reference parsing, frozen expert probing). An optional verbalized self-evolution builds an online rulebook from internal-branch disagreement.

Result: On CrossDomainVAD-12 (12 datasets): macro-AUROC improvements over single-step inference of +6.23 pp (GPT-5.5), +7.93 pp (Seed2.0-lite), +3.52 pp (Qwen3.5-VL-27B). Self-evolution on Qwen3.5-VL-27B yields +2.09 pp mean gain, comparable to oracle-label supervised baseline (+1.99 pp).

Conclusion: Agentic refutation improves anomaly understanding and reasoning of VLMs beyond mere tool output aggregation, enabling effective cross-domain VAD without training.

Abstract: Visual anomaly detection (VAD) is crucial in many real-world fields, such as industrial inspection, medical imaging, infrastructure monitoring, and remote sensing. However, the specific anomaly definitions, data modalities, and annotation standards across different domains make it difficult to transfer single-domain trained VAD models. Vision-language models (VLMs), pre-trained on large-scale cross-domain data, can perform visual perception under task instructions, offering a promising solution for cross-domain VAD. However, single-inference VLM judgments are unreliable, since they rely more on prior knowledge than on normal-sample references or fine-grained feature evidence. We therefore present AnomalyClaw, a training-free VAD agent that turns anomaly judgment into a multi-round refutation process. In each round, the agent proposes candidate anomalies and refutes each against normal-sample references, drawing on a 13-tool library for visual verification, reference parsing, and frozen expert probing. On the CrossDomainVAD-12 benchmark (12 datasets), AnomalyClaw achieves consistent macro-AUROC improvements over single-step direct inference with +6.23 pp on GPT-5.5, +7.93 pp on Seed2.0-lite, and +3.52 pp on Qwen3.5-VL-27B. We further introduce an optional verbalized self-evolution extension. It builds an online rulebook from internal-branch disagreement without oracle labels. On Qwen3.5-VL-27B, it delivers a +2.09 pp mean gain, comparable to a K = 10 oracle-label supervised baseline (+1.99 pp). These results show that agentic refutation improve anomaly understanding and reasoning of VLMs, rather than merely aggregating tool outputs.

</details>


### [272] [Position: Life-Logging Video Streams Make the Privacy-Utility Trade-off Inevitable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10404)
*Tianyuan Zou,Liang Yue,Yang Liu,Ya-Qin Zhang,Sijie Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies privacy-utility trade-offs in life-logging video streams as a foundational challenge for always-on AI systems, calling for pipeline-aware privacy designs and standardized benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Always-on hardware (smart glasses, body cameras) enables persistent AI but introduces severe privacy risks from life-logging video streams revealing sensitive information (behavior, emotions, social interactions) beyond isolated images, which existing attack-specific protections fail to address without utility loss.

Method: The authors propose a position/framework calling for novel pipeline-aware privacy-preserving designs that jointly optimize utility and privacy for long-horizon life-logging visual data, alongside formal privacy metrics and benchmarks.

Result: No empirical results; the paper identifies a research gap and proposes future directions for privacy-preserving life-logging video systems.

Conclusion: The privacy-utility trade-off in life-logging video streams is a foundational challenge for next-generation AI; pipeline-aware designs, formal privacy leakage metrics, and standardized benchmarks are critical open directions for sustainable always-on AI.

Abstract: With the growing prevalence of always-on hardware such as smart glasses, body cameras, and home security systems, life-logging visual sensing is becoming inevitable, forming the backbone of persistent, always-on AI systems. Meanwhile, recent advances in proactive agents and world models signal a fundamental shift from episodic, prompt-driven tools to next-generation AI systems that continuously perceive and react to the physical world. Although life-logging video streams can substantially improve utility of these promising systems, they also introduce significant privacy risks by revealing sensitive information, such as behavioral patterns, emotional states, and social interactions, beyond what isolated images expose. If unresolved, these risks may undermine public trust and hinder the sustainable development of always-on AI technologies. Existing privacy protections are either attack-specific or incur substantial utility loss, and fail to consider the entire data exploitation pipeline. We therefore posit that the privacy-utility trade-off in life-logging video streams is a foundational challenge for next-generation AI systems that demands further investigation. We call for novel pipeline-aware privacy-preserving designs that jointly optimize utility and privacy for long-horizon life-logging visual data. In parallel, formal privacy leakage metrics and standardized benchmarks remain important open directions for future research.

</details>


### [273] [Progressive Photorealistic Simplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10409)
*Adi Rosenthal,Dana Berman,Yedid Hoshen,Ariel Shamir*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces progressive semantic image simplification, a framework that iteratively removes scene elements while preserving photorealism, using VLMs for element selection and a verifier for quality assurance, with applications in decluttering and interactive editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image simplification techniques (NPR-based) sacrifice photographic realism when reducing visual complexity. The authors aim to simplify images while keeping them photorealistic.

Method: A progressive semantic image simplification framework with an iterative Select-Remove-Verify pipeline: VLMs identify/prioritize elements for removal, generative editing removes/inpaints them, and a learned verifier ensures photorealism. A distilled image-to-video model predicts coherent simplification sequences directly.

Result: High-quality simplification trajectories; enables content-aware decluttering, semantic layer decomposition, and interactive editing.

Conclusion: Structured content removal can serve as a practical mechanism for guiding visual interpretation in the photorealistic domain, complementing traditional abstraction methods.

Abstract: Existing image simplification techniques often rely on Non-Photorealistic Rendering (NPR), transforming photographs into stylized sketches, cartoons, or paintings. While effective at reducing visual complexity, such approaches typically sacrifice photographic realism. In this work, we explore a complementary direction: simplifying images while preserving their photorealistic appearance. We introduce progressive semantic image simplification, a framework that iteratively reduces scene complexity by removing and inpainting elements in a controlled manner. At each step, the resulting image remains a plausible natural photograph. Our method combines semantic understanding with generative editing, leveraging Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to identify and prioritize elements for removal, and a learned verifier to ensure photorealism and coherence throughout the process. This is implemented via an iterative Select-Remove-Verify pipeline that produces high-quality simplification trajectories. To improve efficiency, we further distill this process into an image-to-video generation model that directly predicts coherent simplification sequences from a single input image. Beyond generating cleaner and more focused compositions, our approach enables applications such as content-aware decluttering, semantic layer decomposition, and interactive editing. More broadly, our work suggests that simplification through structured content removal can serve as a practical mechanism for guiding visual interpretation within the photorealistic domain, complementing traditional abstraction methods.

</details>


### [274] [CoWorld-VLA: Thinking in a Multi-Expert World Model for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10426)
*Minqing Huang,Yujiao Xiang,Zihan Liang,Jiajie Huang,Jingqi Wang,Zhi Xu,Feiyang Tan,Hangning Zhou,Mu Yang,Gong Che*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoWorld-VLA proposes a multi-expert world reasoning framework for autonomous driving that uses four types of expert tokens (semantic interaction, geometric structure, dynamic evolution, ego trajectory) as explicit conditioning signals for diffusion-based action planning, outperforming on NAVSIM v1 benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA reasoning mechanisms fail to provide planning-oriented intermediate representations: textual CoT cannot preserve continuous spatiotemporal structure, and latent world reasoning is difficult to use as direct conditions for action generation.

Method: CoWorld-VLA extracts complementary world information via multi-source supervision, encodes them into four expert tokens (semantic interaction, geometric structure, dynamic evolution, ego trajectory), and uses a diffusion-based hierarchical multi-expert fusion planner with joint denoising for trajectory generation.

Result: Achieves competitive results on NAVSIM v1 benchmark for future scene generation and planning, with strong collision avoidance and trajectory accuracy. Ablations confirm complementarity of expert tokens.

Conclusion: World representations as explicit conditioning signals effectively guide action planning in autonomous driving, and expert tokens are complementary and effective as planning conditions.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving. However, existing reasoning mechanisms still struggle to provide planning-oriented intermediate representations: textual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fails to preserve continuous spatiotemporal structure, while latent world reasoning remains difficult to use as a direct condition for action generation. In this paper, we propose CoWorld-VLA, a multi-expert world reasoning framework for autonomous driving, where world representations serve as explicit conditions to guide action planning. CoWorld-VLA extracts complementary world information through multi-source supervision and encodes it into expert tokens within the VLA, thereby providing planner-accessible conditioning signals. Specifically, we construct four types of tokens: semantic interaction, geometric structure, dynamic evolution, and ego trajectory tokens, which respectively model interaction intent, spatial structure, future temporal dynamics, and behavioral goals. During action generation, CoWorld-VLA employs a diffusion-based hierarchical multi-expert fusion planner, which is coupled with scene context throughout the joint denoising process to generate continuous ego trajectories. Experiments show that CoWorld-VLA achieves competitive results in both future scene generation and planning on the NAVSIM v1 benchmark, demonstrating strong performance in collision avoidance and trajectory accuracy. Ablation studies further validate the complementarity of expert tokens and their effectiveness as planning conditions for action generation. Code will be available at https://github.com/potatochip1211/CoWorld-VLA.

</details>


### [275] [WorldReasonBench: Human-Aligned Stress Testing of Video Generators as Future World-State Predictors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10434)
*Keming Wu,Yijing Cui,Wenhan Xue,Qijie Wang,Xuan Luo,Zhiyuan Feng,Zuhao Yang,Sudong Wang,Sicong Jiang,Haowei Zhu,Zihan Wang,Ping Nie,Wenhu Chen,Bin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WorldReasonBench evaluates video generation models on world-state reasoning (physical, social, logical, informational consistency) rather than just visual quality, revealing a gap between visual plausibility and true world understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks lack direct testing of whether video generators can reason about how an observed world should evolve over time, despite claims that they are becoming "world simulators."

Method: Reframes evaluation as world-state prediction with 436 curated test cases, 4 reasoning dimensions, 22 subcategories; uses two-part evaluation (Process-aware Reasoning Verification + Multi-dimensional Quality Assessment) and introduces WorldRewardBench with ~6K expert-annotated pairs over 1.4K videos.

Result: Modern video generators show a persistent gap between visual plausibility and world reasoning—videos can look convincing while failing dynamics, causality, or information preservation.

Conclusion: The community needs genuinely world-aware video generation benchmarks; WorldReasonBench and WorldRewardBench will be released to support this goal.

Abstract: Commercial video generation systems such as Seedance2.0 and Veo3.1 have rapidly improved, strengthening the view that video generators may be evolving into "world simulators." Yet the community still lacks a benchmark that directly tests whether a model can reason about how an observed world should evolve over time. We introduce WorldReasonBench, which reframes video generation evaluation as world-state prediction: given an initial state and an action, can a model generate a future video whose state evolution remains physically, socially, logically, and informationally consistent? WorldReasonBench contains 436 curated test cases with structured ground-truth QA annotations spanning four reasoning dimensions and 22 subcategories. We evaluate generated videos with a human-aligned two-part methodology: Process-aware Reasoning Verification uses structured QA and reasoning-phase diagnostics to detect temporal and causal failures, while Multi-dimensional Quality Assessment scores reasoning quality, temporal consistency, and visual aesthetics for ranking and reward modeling. We further introduce WorldRewardBench, a preference benchmark with approximately 6K expert-annotated pairs over 1.4K videos, supporting pair-wise and point-wise reward-model evaluation. Across modern video generators, our results expose a persistent gap between visual plausibility and world reasoning: videos can look convincing while failing dynamics, causality, or information preservation. We will release our benchmarks and evaluation toolkit to support community research on genuinely world-aware video generation at https://github.com/UniX-AI-Lab/WorldReasonBench/.

</details>


### [276] [Filtering Memorization from Parameter-Space in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10439)
*Yu Zhe,Yang Jiayan,Wei Junhao,Yu-Lin Tsai,Wang Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BAF is a training-free, data-free method that filters memorized content from diffusion LoRAs by suppressing spectral channels misaligned with the pretrained backbone's principal subspace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRAs can memorize and reproduce copyrighted/sensitive training images, posing risks in sharing ecosystems where training data and pipelines are unavailable. Existing mitigation methods require access to training data, pipeline, or inference control, which is impractical for post-hoc LoRA weight analysis.

Method: BAF decomposes LoRA updates into spectral channels, measures their alignment with the principal subspace of the pretrained backbone, retains strongly aligned (generalizable) channels, and suppresses weakly aligned (memorization-prone) channels.

Result: Experiments on multiple datasets and diffusion backbones show BAF consistently reduces memorization while preserving or even improving generation quality.

Conclusion: BAF offers an effective, practical solution for post-hoc memorization mitigation in diffusion LoRAs without requiring training data or pipeline access.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become a widely used mechanism for customizing diffusion models, enabling users to inject new visual concepts or styles through lightweight parameter updates. However, LoRAs can memorize training images, causing generated outputs to reproduce copyrighted or sensitive content. This risk is particularly concerning in LoRA-sharing ecosystems, where users distribute trained LoRAs without releasing the underlying training data. Existing approaches for mitigating memorization rely on access to the training pipeline, training data, or control over the inference process, making them difficult to apply when only the released LoRA weights are available. We propose \textbf{Base-Anchored Filtering (BAF)}, a training-free and data-free framework for post-hoc memorization mitigation in diffusion LoRAs. BAF decomposes LoRA updates into spectral channels and measures their alignment with the principal subspace of the pretrained backbone. Channels strongly aligned with this subspace are retained as generalizable adaptations, while weakly aligned channels are suppressed as potential carriers of memorized content. Experiments on multiple datasets and diffusion backbones demonstrate that BAF consistently reduces memorization while preserving or even improving generation quality. Our code is available in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [277] [Uni-Synergy: Bridging Understanding and Generation for Personalized Reasoning via Co-operative Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10445)
*Zijun Shen,Sihan Yang,Ruichuan An,Ziyu Guo,Hao Liang,Ming Lu,Renrui Zhang,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sync-R1 is an end-to-end RL framework with Sync-GRPO and Dynamic Group Scaling that jointly optimizes personalized understanding and generation in UMMs, achieving SOTA on the new UnifyBench++ benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UMMs rely on implicit token-level alignment via supervised fine-tuning, failing to capture the full synergy between personalized understanding and generation tasks.

Method: Proposes Sync-R1 (end-to-end RL framework with explicit reasoning loop), Sync-GRPO (RL method with ensemble reward system), and Dynamic Group Scaling (adaptive filtering of low-potential trajectories). Also introduces UnifyBench++ with denser descriptions and richer contexts.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on personalized understanding and generation, demonstrating superior cross-task reasoning and robust personalization without complex cold-start procedures.

Conclusion: The unified feedback process and integrated reward landscape effectively bridge personalized understanding and generation, with adaptive filtering improving convergence.

Abstract: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) excel in general tasks but struggle to bridge the gap between personalized understanding and generation. Prior works largely rely on implicit token-level alignment via supervised fine-tuning, which fails to fully capture the potential synergy between comprehension and creation. In this work, we propose Sync-R1, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes personalized understanding and generation within a single, explicit reasoning loop. Through this unified feedback process, Sync-R1 enables personalized comprehension to guide content creation, while the resulting generation quality reciprocally refines understanding within an integrated reward landscape. To efficiently orchestrate this dual-task synergy, we introduce Sync-GRPO, a reinforcement learning method utilizing an ensemble reward system. Furthermore, we propose Dynamic Group Scaling (DGS), which adaptively filters low-potential trajectories to reduce gradient variance and accelerate convergence. To better reflect real-world complexity, we introduce UnifyBench++, featuring denser textual descriptions and richer user contexts. Experimental results demonstrate that Sync-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance, showcasing superior cross-task reasoning and robust personalization without requiring complex cold-start procedures. The code and the UnifyBench++ dataset will be released at: https://github.com/arctanxarc/UniCTokens.

</details>


### [278] [Automated high-frequency quantification of fish communities and biomass using computer vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10449)
*Kota Ishikawa,Takuma Masui,Keita Koeda,Rickdane Gomez,Lucas Yutaka Kimura,Michio Kondoh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An automated computer vision framework using stereo cameras and deep learning enables high-frequency, non-invasive quantification of fish community structure (species-level abundance and biomass), demonstrated over 20 days of hourly reef monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fish survey methods (catch-based, visual census, eDNA) lack high-frequency, quantitative observations of abundance and biomass; they are either labor-intensive or unreliable for quantitative estimates.

Method: Custom stereo camera system + deep learning fish identification + multi-object tracking + 3D reconstruction → species-level abundance and biomass estimation.

Result: Applied over 20 days with hourly daytime observations; revealed dynamic fluctuations in species richness, abundance, and biomass driven by compositional changes. Comparisons with visual census and eDNA showed complementary strengths.

Conclusion: The approach provides scalable, continuous, non-invasive, quantitative monitoring of fish communities, advancing capacity to resolve fine-scale temporal dynamics.

Abstract: Quantifying fish community structure is essential for understanding biodiversity and ecosystem responses in a changing environment, yet existing survey methods provide limited high-frequency, quantitative observations. Conventional approaches, including catch-based methods, underwater visual censuses, and environmental DNA metabarcoding, either require intensive labor or lack reliable estimates of abundance and biomass. Here, we develop an automated framework for quantifying fish communities from underwater video using computer vision.
