Ex-vivo experiments demonstrate a mean coverage of 87% and a 53% reduction in procedure time compared with expert manual control.

</details>


### [334] [Hierarchical Prompting with Dual LLM Modules for Robotic Task and Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08330)
*Karolina Źróbek,Tessa Pulli,Paweł Gajewski,Antonio Galiza Cerdeira Gonzalez,Bipin Indurkhya*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical LLM-driven framework for robotic task and motion planning that uses two LLM modules (high-level planning and low-level spatial reasoning) with ReAct-style prompting, achieving 86% success across 24 test scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve natural, intuitive human-robot interaction in service and assistance scenarios through hierarchical language-driven task and motion planning.

Method: Two LLM modules: (1) high-level planning agent using ReAct-style prompts for action sequences, (2) low-level spatial reasoning sub-module for 3D placement interpretation. Integrates YOLOX-GDRNet for object detection and pose estimation.

Result: Overall task success rate of 86% across 24 diverse test scenarios (simple spatial commands, high-level instructions, infeasible requests).

Conclusion: The hierarchical LLM-based approach effectively handles diverse robotic task commands with strong performance, though leaving room for further improvement.

Abstract: We present a hierarchical language-driven framework for robotic task and motion planning to improve natural, intuitive human-robot interaction in service and assistance scenarios. The proposed system employs two large language model (LLM) modules: a high-level planning agent and a low-level spatial reasoning sub-module. The primary agent processes natural language commands and generates action sequences using a ReAct-style prompt, interacting with tools for object perception and manipulation (e.g., pick, place, release). For precise spatial placement, such as interpreting "place the mug next to the plate", a separate sub-prompting module handles 3D reasoning based on object geometry and scene layout. The system integrates YOLOX-GDRNet for object detection and pose estimation, along with a motion execution stub. We evaluated the system in 24 test scenarios, ranging from simple spatial commands to high-level instructions and infeasible requests. The system achieved an overall task success rate of 86%.

</details>


### [335] [Failing Forward: Adaptive Failure-Informed Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08434)
*Meng Zheng,Samhita Marri,Anwesa Choudhuri,Benjamin Planche,Zhongpai Gao,Van Nguyen Nguyen,Terrence Chen,Girish Chowdhary,Ziyan Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AFIL improves VLA robot policies by using failure trajectories as adaptive negative guidance during training and inference, boosting robustness without manual failure design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models trained solely on success behavioral cloning are brittle—small errors cascade into unrecoverable failures due to lack of corrective training signals.

Method: AFIL uses a pretrained VLA to generate failure rollouts online, trains Dual Action Generators (DAGs) for success and failure with a shared vision-language backbone, and adaptively steers action generation away from failure-prone regions with per-step distance-based guidance.

Result: AFIL consistently improves task success rates and robustness over existing VLA baselines across in-domain and out-of-domain short- and long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Failure-aware learning via adaptive negative guidance is an effective, efficient, and general approach to making VLA policies more robust without handcrafted failure design.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models provide a promising paradigm for scalable robotic manipulation, yet their reliance on success-only behavioral cloning leaves them brittle; lacking corrective training signals, minor execution errors rapidly compound into unrecoverable, out-of-distribution failures. To address this limitation, we propose Adaptive Failure-Informed Learning (AFIL), an end-to-end framework that leverages failure trajectories as adaptive negative guidance for diffusion- and flow-based VLA policies. AFIL uses a pretrained VLA to generate failure rollouts online, avoiding the need for handcrafted failure-mode design or human-in-the-loop recovery. It then jointly trains Dual Action Generators (DAGs) for successful and failed behaviors while sharing a common vision-language backbone, enabling efficient failure-aware policy learning with limited parameter overhead. During sampling, the failure generator adaptively steers action generation away from failure-prone regions and toward more reliable success modes, with guidance strength determined by the per-diffusion-step distance between success and failure distributions. Experiments across in-domain and out-of-domain robotic manipulation tasks, covering both short- and long-horizon settings, show that AFIL consistently improves task success rates and robustness over existing VLA baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness, efficiency, and generality.

</details>


### [336] [LE-PAVD: Learning-Enhanced Physics-Aware Vehicle Dynamics for High-Speed Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08489)
*Musabbir Ahmed Arrafi,Malik Ali,Nicholas M. Stiffler,Krishna Bhavithavya Kidambi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LE-PAVD is a hybrid vehicle dynamics model combining physics priors with learned components for high-speed autonomous racing, achieving significant improvements in prediction accuracy (16.1% lower ADE, 20.6% lower FDE, 91.3% lower yaw-rate RMSE) and closed-loop lap times (up to 17.4% faster) while using 21.6% fewer FLOPs and 1.5x faster inference compared to a deep dynamics baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-based vehicle dynamics methods are interpretable but rely on simplifying assumptions, while purely learned models capture nonlinearities yet lack physical consistency and generalization. A hybrid approach is needed to combine the strengths of both for accurate modeling of nonlinear vehicle dynamics in high-speed autonomous racing.

Method: LE-PAVD integrates physics priors with learned components by adding four physics-aware components: load-sensitive Pacejka tire forces, longitudinal load transfer, lateral tire-force effects, and rate-limited actuator inputs. The model is trained end-to-end on simulation and real-world telemetry data.

Result: On an unseen track, LE-PAVD reduces ADE by 16.1%, FDE by 20.6%, and yaw-rate RMSE by 91.3% versus a deep dynamics baseline, while using 21.6% fewer FLOPs and achieving 1.50x faster inference. In closed-loop simulations, it achieves 17.4% faster lap times on a training track and 9.5% on a test track with no boundary violations.

Conclusion: LE-PAVD offers a compact, physics-grounded dynamics backbone that improves predictive fidelity and closed-loop performance while reducing inference cost, making it effective for high-speed autonomous racing applications.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of nonlinear vehicle dynamics is essential for high-speed autonomous racing, where controllers operate at the handling limits. Model-based methods are interpretable but rely on simplifying assumptions, while purely learned models capture nonlinearities yet often lack physical consistency and generalization. We propose LE-PAVD (Learning-Enhanced Physics-Aware Vehicle Dynamics), a hybrid model that integrates physics priors with learned components. Our architecture adds four components: load-sensitive Pacejka tire forces, longitudinal load transfer, lateral tire-force effects, and rate-limited actuator inputs. Trained end-to-end on simulation and real-world telemetry, LE-PAVD enforces physical consistency while improving state prediction accuracy. On an unseen track, LE-PAVD reduces average displacement error (ADE) by 16.1$\%$, final displacement error (FDE) by 20.6$\%$, and lowers yaw-rate root mean squared error (RMSE) by 91.3$\%$ versus a deep dynamics baseline, while using 21.6$\%$ fewer FLOPs and achieving approximately 1.50$\times$ faster inference. In closed-loop simulations, LE-PAVD consistently outperforms the baseline by achieving faster lap times by 17.4$\%$ on a training track and 9.5$\%$ on a test track, without any track boundary violations. Overall, LE-PAVD offers a compact, physics-grounded dynamics backbone that improves predictive fidelity and closed-loop performance while reducing inference cost.

</details>


### [337] [Trajectory-Consistent Flow Matching for Robust Visuomotor Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08511)
*Riad Ahmed,Sujosh Nag,Moniruzzaman Akash,Mostafa Hussein,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes four complementary remedies to address the train-inference mismatch in flow matching policies for robot manipulation, achieving significant improvements on both real-world and simulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard flow matching training optimizes a pointwise velocity objective, but inference requires numerical integration of that field — a mismatch that causes compounding trajectory errors.

Method: Four complementary remedies: (1) auxiliary rectified flow velocity regression, (2) multi-step trajectory consistency training, (3) velocity field regularization, and (4) fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) inference — paired with a dual-view 3D point cloud encoder using two independent PointNet encoders.

Result: On four real-robot tasks, the method achieves 70% and 60% success on long-horizon multi-phase tasks (baselines 0%), and 100% on precision tool placement. Three MetaWorld simulation tasks confirm consistent improvements.

Conclusion: Ablation study confirms components are not independently sufficient; trajectory-level supervision is essential for reliable policy execution.

Abstract: Flow matching policies learn continuous velocity fields that transport noise to actions, enabling fast deterministic inference for robot manipulation. However, standard training optimizes a pointwise velocity objective while inference requires numerical integration of that field -- a mismatch that causes compounding trajectory errors. We propose four complementary remedies: (1) auxiliary rectified flow velocity regression that provides uniform temporal supervision across the full time interval; (2) multi-step trajectory consistency training that supervises the integrated displacement of the velocity field over trajectory segments, directly closing the train-inference gap; (3) velocity field regularization that enforces temporal smoothness, preventing oscillations that destabilize integration; and (4) fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) inference that reduces global discretization error by orders of magnitude over Euler methods. Critically, these components are not independently sufficient -- RK4 without a smooth velocity field fails, and smoothness without trajectory-level supervision still drifts, as our ablation study confirms. We further pair these with a dual-view 3D point cloud encoder using two independent PointNet encoders for complementary spatial perception. On four real-robot tasks across a Franka arm and a Boston Dynamics Spot, our method achieves 70% and 60% overall success on two long-horizon multi-phase tasks where both baselines score 0%, and reaches 100% on precision tool placement. Three MetaWorld simulation tasks confirm consistent improvements, validating that trajectory-level supervision is essential for reliable policy execution.

</details>


### [338] [Model-Reference Adaptive Flight Control of the 95-mg Bee++](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08525)
*Francisco M. F. R. Gonçalves,Conor K. Trygstad,Néstor O. Pérez-Arancibia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A model-reference adaptive control architecture enables high-performance positional tracking for the 95-mg Bee++ flapping-wing aerial vehicle, validated through real-time flight experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve high-performance positional tracking for insect-scale flapping-wing aerial vehicles (the Bee++), which are inherently challenging to control due to their small size and complex dynamics.

Method: Model-reference adaptive control (MRAC) architecture designed for the 95-mg Bee++ flapping-wing aerial vehicle.

Result: Demonstrated suitability, functionality, and high performance through real-time flight experiments.

Conclusion: The MRAC approach is effective and high-performing for positional tracking of insect-scale flapping-wing aerial vehicles like the Bee++.

Abstract: We introduce a model-reference adaptive control (MRAC) architecture for high-performance positional tracking of the Bee++, a 95-mg insect-scale flapping-wing aerial vehicle. The suitability, functionality, and high performance of the proposed approach are demonstrated using data from real-time flight experiments.

</details>


### [339] [BEACON: Cross-Domain Co-Training of Generative Robot Policies via Best-Effort Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08571)
*Antong Zhang,Han Qi,Heng Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BEACON is a theory-driven framework for cross-domain co-training of robot policies that uses discrepancy-aware importance reweighting to adapt diffusion-based visuomotor policies from abundant source to limited target demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Train robot policies with abundant source domain data but only limited target domain data, addressing the domain gap without requiring explicit alignment.

Method: Casts cross-domain co-training as discrepancy-aware importance-reweighting; jointly learns diffusion-based visuomotor policy and per-sample source weights using scalable instance-level discrepancy estimators and stochastic alternating updates.

Result: Outperforms target-only, fixed-ratio co-training, and feature-alignment baselines in sim-to-sim, sim-to-real, and multi-source manipulation settings; achieves implicit feature alignment.

Conclusion: Discrepancy-aware importance reweighting is a practical and effective approach for cross-domain robot policy learning, even without explicit alignment objectives.

Abstract: We introduce BEACON--Best-Effort Adaptation for Cross-Domain Co-Training--a theory-driven framework for training generative robot policies with abundant source demonstrations and limited target demonstrations. BEACON casts cross-domain co-training as a discrepancy-aware importance-reweighting problem, jointly learning a diffusion-based visuomotor policy and per-sample source weights that minimize an objective informed by target-domain generalization guarantees. To make best-effort adaptation practical for high-dimensional sequence policies, we develop scalable instance-level discrepancy estimators, stochastic alternating updates for policy and weights, and a multi-source extension that balances heterogeneous source domains. Across sim-to-sim, sim-to-real, and multi-source manipulation settings, BEACON improves robustness and data efficiency over target-only, fixed-ratio co-training, and feature-alignment baselines. Importantly, even without an explicit alignment objective, BEACON achieves feature alignment as an implicit result of discrepancy-aware cross-domain co-training.

</details>


### [340] [ATAAT: Adaptive Threat-Aware Adversarial Tuning Framework against Backdoor Attacks on Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08612)
*Kewei Chen,Yayu Long,Shuai Li,Mingsheng Shang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper identifies "Gradient Interference" as the root cause of failed backdoor attacks on VLA models and proposes ATAAT, an adaptive framework that uses threat-aware gradient decoupling to achieve >80% attack success with only 5% poisoning rate while maintaining extreme stealth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Escalating security vulnerabilities in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, particularly the failure of traditional backdoor attack paradigms due to "Gradient Interference."

Method: An Adaptive Threat-Aware Adversarial Tuning (ATAAT) framework with a "Threat-Method Adaptive Mapping" mechanism that intelligently selects optimal gradient decoupling strategies based on adversary capabilities.

Result: Achieves >80% Targeted Attack Success Rate (TASR) with only 5% poisoning rate, handles complex semantic-level triggers, and enables implicit decoupled attacks in data poisoning scenarios.

Conclusion: Reveals a critical security vulnerability in VLAs and provides theoretical and methodological foundations for future defense architectures.

Abstract: Addressing the escalating security vulnerabilities in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, this study investigates backdoor attacks targeting the visual pathway. We identify a core obstacle causing the failure of traditional attack paradigms: "Gradient Interference." This phenomenon represents an optimization failure triggered by conflicting strategies during end-to-end training. To resolve this, we propose an Adaptive Threat-Aware Adversarial Tuning (ATAAT) framework. Through its core "Threat-Method Adaptive Mapping" mechanism, ATAAT intelligently selects the optimal gradient decoupling strategy based on the adversary's capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ATAAT exhibits significant advantages, achieving a highly robust Targeted Attack Success Rate (TASR > 80%) while maintaining extreme stealthiness with merely a 5% poisoning rate. It efficiently handles complex semantic-level triggers and achieves implicit decoupled attacks in data poisoning scenarios for the first time. This work reveals a critical security vulnerability in VLAs and provides theoretical and methodological support for future defense architectures.

</details>


### [341] [Geometry Guided Self-Consistency for Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08638)
*Yinwei Dai,Zhuofu Chen,Lijie Yang,Ravi Netravali*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KeyStone improves diffusion-based physical AI action generation by sampling K candidate action chunks in parallel, clustering them, and returning the medoid of the largest cluster, boosting task success rates by up to 13.3% with no extra latency or training cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-trajectory sampling in diffusion-based action generation is brittle due to inherent stochasticity, and this brittleness compounds across sequential rounds in an episode.

Method: KeyStone draws K candidate action chunks in parallel (leveraging spare compute from memory-bandwidth-bound diffusion), clusters them by Euclidean distance in continuous action space, and returns the medoid of the largest cluster without requiring a learned judge model.

Result: Improves task success rates by up to 13.3% over single-trajectory sampling across VLAs and WAMs with negligible latency overhead, and matches model-based selectors at no training cost.

Conclusion: Self-consistency via parallel candidate sampling and geometric clustering is a practical, zero-cost inference-time improvement for diffusion-based physical AI action generation.

Abstract: State-of-the-art physical AI models generate a chunk of actions per inference through diffusion or flow matching, iteratively refining an initial noise sample into an action trajectory. Because this inference process is inherently stochastic, committing to a single trajectory per round is brittle, and this brittleness compounds across the many sequential rounds that comprise a complete episode. We introduce KeyStone, an inference-time self-consistency method for diffusion-based action generation that draws $K$ candidate action chunks in parallel from a shared model context, clusters them in continuous action space, and returns the medoid of the largest cluster -- no additional model required. Two properties make this practical. First, the compact nature of action trajectories makes diffusion inference memory-bandwidth bound, leaving spare compute capacity to run $K$ chains in parallel with no additional wall-clock latency. Second, unlike token or pixel spaces where distance carries no semantic meaning and selection requires a learned judge, action chunks are geometrically structured such that Euclidean distance directly reflects physical similarity, making selection principled and judge-free. Across diverse vision-language-action models (VLAs) and world-action models (WAMs), KeyStone improves task success rates by up to \textbf{13.3\%} over single-trajectory sampling with negligible latency overhead, while having on par accuracy with model-based selectors at no training cost. We open source KeyStone at https://github.com/dywsjtu/keystone.

</details>


### [342] [REAP: Reinforcement-Learning End-to-End Autonomous Parking with Gaussian Splatting Simulator for Real2Sim2Real Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08713)
*Changze Li,Zhe Chen,Shaoyu Chen,Lisen Mu,Yijian Li,Yuelong Yu,Qian Zhang,Qing Su,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: REAP is an end-to-end reinforcement learning method for autonomous parking that combines SAC with asymmetric learning, behavior cloning from rule-based planners, and a soft predictive collision penalty, trained in a Real2Sim2Real 3DGS simulator to handle extreme parking scenarios like narrow mechanical slots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional parking methods use a multistage approach lacking holistic optimization, causing failures in extreme scenarios (mechanical/dead-end slots). End-to-end methods using imitation learning are limited by data cost/distribution coverage, while RL suffers from inefficient exploration.

Method: REAP uses Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) in an asymmetric RL framework, distills a rule-based planner via behavior cloning for faster convergence, introduces a soft predictive collision penalty mechanism, and trains in a Real2Sim2Real simulator built with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS).

Result: REAP successfully parks in various parking space types, particularly demonstrating feasibility of end-to-end RL parking in extremely narrow mechanical slots when deployed on physical vehicles.

Conclusion: End-to-end RL with asymmetric training, behavior cloning, and a realistic 3DGS simulator can effectively address extreme parking scenarios that challenge traditional multistage methods.

Abstract: In recent years, autonomous parking has made significant advances, yet parking tasks still face challenges in extreme scenarios such as mechanical and dead-end parking slots, often resulting in failures. This is mainly due to traditional parking methods adopting a multistage approach, lacking the ability to optimize the parking problem as a whole. End-to-end methods enable joint optimization across perception and planning modules to eliminate the accumulation of errors, enhancing algorithm performance in extreme scenarios. Although several end-to-end parking methods use imitation or reinforcement learning, the former is limited by data cost and distribution coverage, while the latter suffers from inefficient exploration. To address these challenges, we propose a Reinforcement learning End-to-end Autonomous Parking method (REAP). REAP employs Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) within an asymmetric reinforcement learning framework to improve training efficiency and inference performance. To accelerate model convergence, we distill the capabilities of a rule-based planner into the end-to-end network through behavior cloning. We further introduce a soft predictive collision penalty mechanism to reduce collision rates by penalizing obstacle-approaching actions. To ensure that the trained reinforcement learning network can directly transfer to real-world scenarios, we have established a Real2Sim2Real simulator. In the Real2Sim step, we use 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to transform real-world scenes into digital scenes. In the Sim2Real step, we deploy the end-to-end model onto the vehicle to bridge the Sim2Real gap. Trained in the 3DGS simulator and deployed on physical vehicles, REAP successfully parks in various types of parking spaces, especially demonstrating the feasibility of end-to-end RL parking in extremely narrow mechanical slots.

</details>


### [343] [HULK: Large-scale Hierarchical Coordination under Continual and Uncertain Temporal Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08722)
*Qingyuan Luo,Jie Li,Meng Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes HULK, a hierarchical framework for coordinating large-scale multi-agent systems under continual, uncertain temporal tasks specified as temporal logic formulas. It uses rolling assignment of tasks to subteams and dynamic intra-subteam coordination, improving computational efficiency and robustness over offline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work assumes static, known tasks solvable via offline integer programming. However, real-world applications (e.g., delivery, search and rescue) involve online-generated tasks with uncertain numbers of subtasks, making offline solutions impractical due to constant re-computation and global communication needs.

Method: HULK is a two-layer hierarchical framework: (1) an upper layer performs rolling assignment of currently known tasks to subteams within a finite horizon; (2) a lower layer handles dynamic coordination within each subteam based on detected subtasks during online execution. Tasks are specified as temporal logic formulas over collaborative actions.

Result: The method is rigorously validated on large-scale heterogeneous systems under various temporal tasks and environmental uncertainties, demonstrating improved computational efficiency and robustness compared to offline approaches.

Conclusion: Hierarchical coordination at different granularities and triggering conditions effectively addresses the challenges of continual, uncertain temporal tasks in large-scale multi-agent systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems can be extremely efficient when working concurrently and collaboratively, e.g., for delivery, surveillance, search and rescue. Coordination of such teams often involves two aspects: selecting appropriate subteams for different tasks in various areas, and coordinating agents in the subteams to execute the associated subtasks. Existing work often assumes that the tasks are static and known beforehand, where an integer program can be formulated and solved offline. However, in many applications, the team-wise tasks are generated online continually by external requests, and the amount of subtasks within each task is uncertain, e.g., the number of packages to deliver or victims to rescue. The aforementioned offline solution becomes inadequate as it would require constant re-computation for the whole team and global communication to broadcast the results. Thus, this work tackles the large-scale coordination problem under continual and uncertain temporal tasks, specified as temporal logic formulas over collaborative actions. The proposed hierarchical framework, HULK, consists of two interleaved layers: the rolling assignment of currently known tasks to subteams within a certain horizon, and the dynamic coordination within a subteam given the detected subtasks during online execution. Thus, coordination is performed hierarchically at different granularities and triggering conditions, improving computational efficiency and robustness. The method is validated rigorously over large-scale heterogeneous systems under various temporal tasks and environment uncertainties.

</details>


### [344] [Latent Geometry Beyond Search: Amortizing Planning in World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08732)
*Hoang Nguyen,Xiaohao Xu,Xiaonan Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper replaces expensive online planning in latent world models with a lightweight Goal-Conditioned Inverse Dynamics Model (GC-IDM), achieving 100-130x speedup while matching or exceeding performance of CEM and other planners across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate when learned representations simplify control (not just enable prediction), motivated by the computational cost of goal-oriented planning in latent spaces.

Method: Uses a pretrained LeWorldModel with smooth/regularized latent geometry, then amortizes planning into a lightweight GC-IDM that maps (current latent, goal latent, horizon) directly to next action, bypassing iterative search.

Result: Matches/exceeds CEM in 7/8 environment-protocol settings; reduces per-decision cost by 100-130x; outperforms CEM, MPPI, iCEM, and gradient-based planners consistently.

Conclusion: Sufficiently structured latent spaces can shift planning burden from online optimization to learned inference, as much of the structure found by test-time planners is already locally encoded in the latent representation.

Abstract: Modern vision-based world models can represent observations as compact yet expressive latent manifolds, but fast goal-oriented planning in these spaces remains challenging. This raises a central question: when does a learned representation simplify control, rather than merely enabling prediction? We study this question in a pretrained LeWorldModel, whose latent geometry is regularized for smoothness and uniformity. Our key insight is that, under such geometry, planning can be amortized into a latent inverse-dynamics mapping instead of requiring online search. We therefore replace iterative planning with a lightweight Goal-Conditioned Inverse Dynamics Model (GC-IDM) that maps the current latent state, goal latent state, and remaining horizon directly to the next action. Empirically, across four benchmark environments spanning navigation, contact-rich manipulation, and continuous control, our controller matches or exceeds CEM in seven of eight environment-protocol settings while reducing per-decision cost by 100-130x. A broader sweep over test-time planners (CEM, MPPI, iCEM, and gradient-based methods) shows that this result is not specific to a particular optimizer. These findings suggest that much of the structure recovered by test-time planning is already locally encoded in the latent representation. More broadly, our results indicate that sufficiently structured latent spaces can shift part of the planning burden from online optimization to learned inference.

</details>


### [345] [A Visuo-Tactile Data Collection System with Haptic Feedback for Coarse-to-Fine Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08757)
*Yeseung Kim,Nayoung Oh,Jun Park,Teetat Thamronglak,Daehyung Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A visuo-tactile data-collection system with direct-drive gripper preserving haptic feedback, tactile arrays, and real-time temporal annotation for generating contact-rich demonstrations for imitation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional demonstration systems decouple the operator from contact forces, hindering the demonstration of subtle force modulation needed for contact-rich manipulation tasks.

Method: A direct-drive gripper actuated by operator fingers (preserving haptic feedback), integrated visual sensors, custom tactile arrays (capturing contact geometry), and a push button for real-time temporal task annotation.

Result: Produces multimodal datasets that fuse in-hand force perception with temporal structure, designed for coarse-to-fine learning algorithms exploiting structural task knowledge.

Conclusion: The system enables the development of high-quality manipulation policies by preserving natural force feedback and temporal structure in demonstrations.

Abstract: We present a visuo-tactile data-collection system that generates temporally structured, contact-rich demonstrations for imitation learning. Conventional systems often decouple the operator from contact forces, which hinders the demonstration of subtle force modulation. Our system introduces a direct-drive gripper that the operator actuates with the fingers, preserving natural haptic feedback. Integrated visual sensors and custom tactile arrays capture image streams and contact geometry. A handle-mounted push button enables the operator to annotate the task's temporal structure in real time by marking task-critical regions. By fusing in-hand force perception with in-situ temporal annotation, the system produces multimodal datasets designed for coarse-to-fine learning algorithms that exploit structural task knowledge, enabling the development of high-quality manipulation policies.

</details>


### [346] [Omni-scale Learning-based Sequential Decision Framework for Order Fulfillment of Tote-handling Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08758)
*Jiaxin Liu,Peng Yang,Yuping Li,Xinyue Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes OLSF-TRS, a generalized sequential decision framework combining combinatorial optimization with multi-agent RL for order fulfillment in tote-handling robotic systems, achieving near-optimal (≤3.5% gap) small-scale and 8-30%+ improvement over baselines at large scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: E-commerce expansion drives shift from pallets to totes in intralogistics, but existing order-fulfillment decision mechanisms are system-specific and not generalizable or transferable across different tote-handling robotic systems.

Method: Omni-scale Learning-based Sequential Decision Framework (OLSF-TRS) combining structured combinatorial optimization with multi-agent reinforcement learning to coordinate order, tote, and robot decisions in a unified manner.

Result: Near-optimal with ≤3.5% optimality gaps on small-scale systems; 8-12% and 30%+ reduction in tote movements vs. heuristic and SOTA rule-based baselines on large-scale systems; real-time responsiveness maintained.

Conclusion: OLSF-TRS delivers an efficient, unified, and generalizable order fulfillment decision framework for tote-handling robotic systems, enabling cost reduction, lower energy consumption, and enhanced throughput stability for e-commerce and industrial logistics.

Abstract: Driven by the rapid expansion of e-commerce and small-batch production, the size of the intralogistics load unit of finished goods, semi-finished goods and raw materials is steadily shrinking. Totes are gradually replacing pallets as the primary handling and storage container. This shift has propelled tote-handling robotic systems to the forefront of automation order fulfillment centers. The order-fulfillment decisions of tote-handling robotic systems share a common order-tote-robot sequential decision-making nature. Existing studies primarily focus on decision mechanisms tailored to particular systems, making it difficult to generalize or transfer them to other contexts. We propose an Omni-scale Learning-based Sequential Decision Framework for Order Fulfillment of Tote-handling Robotic Systems (OLSF-TRS), a generalized and scalable sequential decision framework that combines structured combinatorial optimization with multi-agent reinforcement learning to coordinate order,tote, and robot decisions. On small-scale tote-handling robotic systems, OLSF-TRS achieves near-optimal performance with average optimality gaps below 3.5% across two distinct system configurations. In large-scale scenarios, OLSF-TRS consistently outperforms heuristic baselines across two different system types, reducing total tote movements by 8-12% and over 30% compared to SOTA rule-based approaches, while maintaining real-time responsiveness. These improvements translate into tangible operational benefits, including cost reduction, lower energy consumption, and enhanced throughput stability. The proposed framework delivers an efficient and unified order fulfillment decision-making framework for widely deployed tote-handling robotic systems,supporting high-quality order fulfillment in both e-commerce and industrial logistics sectors.

</details>


### [347] [ProcVLM: Learning Procedure-Grounded Progress Rewards for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08774)
*Youhe Feng,Hansen Shi,Haoyang Li,Xinlei Guo,Yang Wang,Chengyang Zhang,Jinkai Zhang,Xiaohan Zhang,Jie Tang,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ProcVLM is a vision-language model that learns procedure-grounded progress as a dense reward signal for long-horizon robotic manipulation, using frame-level subtask annotations and visual change to estimate task progress more accurately than trajectory-level or time-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward models rely on trajectory-level success labels or time-based interpolation, which conflate elapsed time with true task progress and fail to capture unfinished steps, stagnation, and failure states in long-horizon manipulation tasks.

Method: ProcVLM grounds progress estimation in procedural structure and intra-stage visual change, adopts reasoning-before-estimation (inferring remaining atomic actions first), synthesizes frame-level subtask-semantic annotations with progress budgets based on subtask structure and intra-subtask visual change, and pretrains on ProcCorpus-60M (30 embodied datasets, 60M frames) with ProcVQA for procedure-aware pretraining.

Result: ProcVLM improves embodied procedural reasoning and yields more discriminative trajectory-internal progress estimates than representative baselines on ProcVQA and reward-model benchmarks, supporting its use as dense reward model for downstream reward-guided policy optimization.

Conclusion: Procedure-grounded progress estimation with reasoning-before-estimation paradigm provides more accurate and discriminative dense reward signals for long-horizon robotic manipulation compared to existing approaches.

Abstract: Long-horizon robotic manipulation requires dense feedback that reflects how a task advances through its procedural stages, not merely whether the final outcome is successful. Existing reward models often rely on trajectory-level success labels or time-based interpolation, which can conflate elapsed time with true task progress and therefore fail to capture unfinished steps, stagnation, and failure states. We present ProcVLM, a progress-aware vision-language model that learns procedure-grounded progress as a dense reward signal for manipulation. Rather than deriving progress from terminal outcomes or temporal proxies, ProcVLM grounds progress estimation in procedural structure and intra-stage visual change, and further adopts a reasoning-before-estimation paradigm that infers the remaining atomic actions before estimating task progress. Specifically, we construct this supervision by synthesizing frame-level subtask-semantic annotations, assigning progress budgets according to subtask structure, and distributing each budget based on intra-subtask visual change. To train ProcVLM at scale, we build a standardized procedural supervision synthesis pipeline and construct ProcCorpus-60M from 30 embodied datasets with 60M annotated frames, from which we derive ProcVQA for procedure-aware pretraining, with progress estimation as the central task alongside action segmentation and future planning. Experiments on ProcVQA and reward-model benchmarks show that ProcVLM improves embodied procedural reasoning and yields more discriminative trajectory-internal progress estimates than representative baselines, supporting its use as a dense reward model for downstream reward-guided policy optimization. Project page: https://procvlm.github.io/

</details>


### [348] [ElasticFlow: One-Step Physics-Consistent Policy with Elastic Time Horizons for Language-Guided Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08799)
*Kewei Chen,Yayu Long,Shuai Li,Mingsheng Shang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ElasticFlow is a distillation-free, physics-consistent one-step policy framework that enables efficient (71Hz) inference by modeling the average velocity field from Mean Field Theory, with an Elastic Time Horizons mechanism to handle temporal heterogeneity in robotic tasks, outperforming SOTA methods like OpenVLA and π₀ on long-horizon benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies suffer from high latency due to iterative denoising, and existing acceleration methods often sacrifice physical consistency.

Method: Reconstructs Mean Field Theory by modeling the average velocity field for direct single-step noise-to-action mapping, and introduces Elastic Time Horizons to encode control granularity and overcome Spectral Bias.

Result: Achieves ~71Hz 1-NFE inference, and outperforms OpenVLA and π₀ on long-horizon tasks in LIBERO, CALVIN, and RoboTwin benchmarks.

Conclusion: ElasticFlow enables efficient, robust, and semantically aligned control for embodied AI tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion policies have demonstrated exceptional performance in embodied AI. However, their iterative denoising process results in high latency, and existing acceleration methods often sacrifice physical consistency. To address this, we propose ElasticFlow, a distillation-free, physics-consistent one-step policy framework. We reconstruct the Mean Field Theory by directly modeling the average velocity field, enabling a direct single-step mapping from noise to action. Addressing the Temporal Heterogeneity of robotic tasks, we introduce the Elastic Time Horizons mechanism. This mechanism effectively overcomes Spectral Bias by explicitly encoding control granularity, achieving efficient alignment between semantic instructions and physical execution horizons. Experiments on benchmarks such as LIBERO, CALVIN, and RoboTwin demonstrate that ElasticFlow achieves efficient 1-NFE inference (approximately 71Hz). Furthermore, it outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including OpenVLA and $π_0$, on long-horizon tasks, highlighting its potential for efficient, robust, and semantically aligned control.

</details>


### [349] [Constraint-Aware Diffusion Priors for High-Fidelity and Versatile Quadruped Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08804)
*Jianhui Chen,Ruixin Zhan,Liu Liu,Yang Cai,Ziqiao Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diff-CAST uses diffusion models to replace GAN-based discriminators in quadruped locomotion RL, combined with symmetric command conditioning and constrained RL for safe, drift-free, multi-modal motion learning from large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAN-based discriminators suffer from mode collapse when scaling to massive, uncurated multi-source datasets, and kinematic priors cause OOD heading drifts and safety risks on hardware.

Method: Proposes Diff-CAST, which (1) replaces GAN discriminators with diffusion models for multi-modal distribution modeling of stylistic rewards, (2) uses Symmetric Augmented Command Conditioning (SACC) for drift-free tracking, and (3) integrates Constrained RL for hardware safety.

Result: Diff-CAST mitigates mode collapse, enables seamless skill transitions, and ensures robust, hardware-compliant locomotion on a real quadruped.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based discriminators effectively scale RL+imitation frameworks to heterogeneous datasets while maintaining safety and tracking fidelity in real-world deployment.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning combined with imitation learning has significantly advanced biomimetic quadrupedal locomotion. However, scaling these frameworks to massive, multi-source datasets exposes fundamental bottlenecks. First, traditional GAN-based discriminators are prone to mode collapse, struggling to capture diverse motion distributions from uncurated datasets. Second, existing kinematic priors suffer from out-of-distribution (OOD) tracking conflicts, leading to severe unintended heading drifts during complex maneuvers. Furthermore, deploying unconstrained priors to physical hardware poses critical safety risks by disregarding actuator dynamics. To overcome these challenges, we propose Diff-CAST (Diffusion-guided Constraint-Aware Symmetric Tracking), a novel motion prior framework leveraging the multi-modal distribution modeling capabilities of diffusion models for stylistic rewards. Diff-CAST effectively replaces traditional GAN discriminators, unlocking robust data scaling on heterogeneous collections. To ensure high-fidelity intent execution and reliable real-world deployment, we introduce a comprehensive Sim2Re architecture integrating Symmetric Augmented Command Conditioning (SACC) for drift-free tracking, and Constrained RL for hardware safety. Experiments on a quadruped demonstrate that Diff-CAST mitigates mode collapse, enables seamless transitions between diverse skills, and ensures robust, hardware-compliant locomotion.

</details>


### [350] [AssemPlanner: A Multi-Agent Based Task Planning Framework for Flexible Assembly System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08831)
*Chenhao Zhang,Chaoran Zhang,Zhaobo Xu,Yongbo Yang,Pingfa Feng,Long Zeng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AssemPlanner is a multi-agent task planning framework for flexible assembly systems that converts natural language task descriptions into actionable production operations using specialized agents (SchedAgent, KnowledgeAgent, LineBalanceAgent) and a scene graph, with ReAct-based adaptive reasoning to replace traditional static pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing task planning methods for flexible assembly systems require time-consuming multi-expert configuration to establish production lines for new products, creating a bottleneck in adapting to product changeovers.

Method: The framework uses multiple specialized agents: SchedAgent (central ReAct-based reasoning engine that adaptively adjusts actions via multi-agent feedback), KnowledgeAgent, LineBalanceAgent, and a scene graph. It takes natural language task descriptions and converts them into sequential production operations.

Result: The proposed framework autonomously resolves complex industrial process constraints by observing feedback from the specialized agents and scene graph, enabling adaptive task planning without static pipelines.

Conclusion: AssemPlanner offers a more flexible and efficient approach to task planning in assembly systems by replacing time-consuming expert configuration with multi-agent collaboration, and the released code/dataset supports reproducibility.

Abstract: In flexible assembly systems, existing task planning methods require a time-consuming configuration process by multiple experts to establish a production line for a new product. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-agent based task planning framework for flexible assembly systems, denoted as AssemPlanner. It takes tasks described in natural language as input, which are then converted into actionable sequential production operations. It comprises several specialized agents, including SchedAgent , KnowledgeAgent, LineBalanceAgent, and a scene graph. Within the proposed framework, SchedAgent serves as the central reasoning engine. Departing from traditional static pipelines, AssemPlanner utilizes a ReAct-based SchedAgent to adaptively adjust actions via multi-agent feedback. By observing the feedback from KnowledgeAgent, LineBalanceAgent, and the scene graph, it autonomously resolves complex industrial process constraints. To facilitate reproducibility, all code and datasets are released at https://github.com/chz332/Assemplanner.

</details>


### [351] [Preserving Foundational Capabilities in Flow-Matching VLAs through Conservative SFT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08879)
*Tianyi Zhang,Shaopeng Zhai,Haoran Zhang,Fuxian Huang,Qi Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ConSFT is a conservative fine-tuning method for flow-matching VLA models that prevents catastrophic forgetting by dynamically scaling gradients based on model confidence, achieving over 20% better capability retention than vanilla SFT without needing prior data or extra networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unconstrained fine-tuning of flow-matching VLA models causes dense parameter overwrites that degrade pre-trained capabilities, motivating the need for an optimization objective that prevents catastrophic forgetting without requiring prior data or architectural modifications.

Method: Conservative Supervised Fine-Tuning (ConSFT) dynamically scales learning signals based on model confidence, suppressing excessive gradients from low-confidence samples to prevent disproportionate parameter updates, inspired by reinforcement learning's trust-region clipping.

Result: ConSFT outperforms vanilla SFT in capability retention by over 20% absolute margin on LIBERO and RoboTwin benchmarks across three VLAs (π₀, π₀.₅, GR00T-N1.6-3B), matching Experience Replay's efficacy without prior data. Real-world deployments confirm no spatial overfitting.

Conclusion: ConSFT effectively bounds parameter disruption risk during fine-tuning, preserving pre-trained physical skills while acquiring new target tasks, achieving data-free conservative fine-tuning for flow-matching VLA models.

Abstract: Unconstrained fine-tuning of flow-matching Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models drives dense parameter overwrites, degrading pre-trained capabilities. We present Conservative Supervised Fine-Tuning (ConSFT), an optimization objective that adapts to target distributions while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, requiring zero prior data or architectural overhead. By dynamically scaling learning signals based on model confidence, ConSFT suppresses excessive gradients from low-confidence samples to prevent disproportionate parameter updates, thereby bounding the intrinsic parameter disruption risk. Inspired by reinforcement learning's trust-region clipping, this formulation establishes a progressive learning dynamic to secure target convergence and prior capability retention, maintaining sparse parameter updates without relying on the parallel reference networks required by explicit regularization. We evaluate ConSFT on the LIBERO and RoboTwin benchmarks across state-of-the-art flow-matching VLAs ($π_0$, $π_{0.5}$, and GR00T-N1.6-3B). The method outperforms vanilla SFT in capability retention by an average absolute margin of over 20\%, matching the efficacy of data-heavy Experience Replay in a prior-data-free regime. Real-world robotic deployments confirm that ConSFT precludes spatial overfitting during downstream adaptation, preserving pre-trained physical skills while acquiring sequential target tasks.

</details>


### [352] [Raymoval: Raycasting-based Dynamic Object Removal for Static 3D Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08937)
*Daebeom Kim,Seungjae Lee,Seoyeon Jang,Kevin Christiansen Marsim,Hyun Myung*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a raycasting-based module for removing dynamic objects from static 3D maps, using azimuth-elevation projection and consistency tests to produce cleaner static maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic objects leave residual traces and cause surface loss in static 3D mapping, reducing map consistency for long-term robot navigation.

Method: Projects scans onto azimuth-elevation grid, compares bin-wise minimum range with raycasted first-hit distance, applies raycast consistency test to separate dynamic/static points, then refines labels via spatial consistency validation.

Result: Demonstrates consistent static mapping results with lower residual dynamics and reduced over-removal on SemanticKITTI and a custom dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed raycasting-based approach effectively removes dynamic objects from 3D static maps while minimizing over-removal, improving map consistency for autonomous navigation.

Abstract: Static mapping is fundamental to robot navigation, providing a persistent geometric prior and a consistent reference for long-term autonomy. However, dynamic objects leave residual traces and cause surface loss, which reduces map consistency. We propose a raycasting-based module for dynamic object removal in static 3D mapping. Each scan is projected onto an azimuth-elevation grid, and for every viewing direction we compare the bin-wise minimum range with the map's first-hit distance computed by raycasting. Furthermore, we apply a raycast consistency test that separates dynamic from static points. Finally, a spatial consistency validation step refines labels, producing static maps with lower residual dynamics and reduced over-removal. We evaluate our approach quantitatively and qualitatively on SemanticKITTI and a challenging custom dataset, and show consistent static mapping results.

</details>


### [353] [A low-cost mockup to simulate robotic laser cutting in nuclear decommissioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08947)
*Frederico Fernandes Afonso Silva,Murilo Marques Marinho,Bruno Vilhena Adorno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost UV-based laser cutting mockup for nuclear decommissioning, using adaptive control and beam-steering to achieve ~2.4mm tracking accuracy without calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simulate laser cutting of containers in nuclear decommissioning with a low-cost, uncalibrated system that avoids costly calibration procedures in hazardous environments.

Method: A three-axis table + 6-DOF manipulator with UV torch, visual fiducial markers, constrained task-space adaptive motion controller for compensation and collision avoidance, controlling the UV beam (4-DOF) rather than full end-effector pose.

Result: Mean tracking accuracy of 3.9 mm (SD 2.5) for end-effector control and 2.4 mm (SD 1.3) for UV beam control.

Conclusion: The proposed uncalibrated system effectively tracks laser cutting trajectories with improved accuracy when controlling the beam instead of the full end-effector pose.

Abstract: This paper introduces a low-cost experimental mockup to simulate the laser cutting process of containers in nuclear decommissioning. It is composed of a three-axis table supporting a cuboid container with ultraviolet-sensitive faces, a six-degree-of-freedom serial manipulator holding an ultraviolet torch that simulates the laser, and a visual system based on cameras and fiducial markers. The system employs a constrained task-space adaptive motion controller that compensates for inaccurate parameters and eliminates the need to calibrate the system. Furthermore, as the motion controller explicitly accounts for geometric constraints, the robot reactively avoids collisions with obstacles while handling the ultraviolet torch. To enhance tracking of the laser-cutting path, we control the ultraviolet beam, which requires only four degrees of freedom, instead of the full end-effector pose. Experiments show that, despite an initially uncalibrated system, the overall system is capable of tracking different trajectories with an overall mean accuracy of 3.9 (sd 2.5) mm when the end-effector pose is controlled and 2.4 (sd 1.3) mm when the ultraviolet beam is controlled.

</details>


### [354] [Towards Backdoor-Based Ownership Verification for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09005)
*Ming Sun,Rui Wang,Xingrui Yu,Lihua Jing,Hangyu Du,Zhenglin Wan,Xu Pan,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GuardVLA is the first backdoor-based ownership verification framework for Vision-Language-Action models, embedding stealthy watermarks into embodied visual data and using a swap-and-detect mechanism for reliable post-release ownership verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As VLAs are increasingly shared and adapted, protecting model ownership becomes essential for secure deployment and responsible open-source usage.

Method: Embeds a stealthy backdoor watermark via secret messages in embodied visual data during training; uses a swap-and-detect mechanism with trigger projector and external classifier head for verification.

Result: Enables reliable ownership verification while preserving benign task performance; the watermark remains detectable under post-release model adaptation.

Conclusion: GuardVLA provides an effective and robust solution for VLA ownership verification.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) support generalist robotic control by enabling end-to-end decision policies directly from multi-modal inputs. As trained VLAs are increasingly shared and adapted, protecting model ownership becomes essential for secure deployment and responsible open-source usage. In this paper, we present GuardVLA, the first backdoor-based ownership verification framework specifically designed for VLAs. GuardVLA embeds a stealthy and harmless backdoor watermark into the protected model during training by injecting secret messages into embodied visual data. For post-release verification, we propose a swap-and-detect mechanism, in which the trigger projector and an external classifier head are used to activate and detect the embedded backdoor based on prediction probabilities. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, model architectures, and adaptation settings demonstrate that GuardVLA enables reliable ownership verification while preserving benign task performance. Further results show that the embedded watermark remains detectable under post-release model adaptation.

</details>


### [355] [Terminal Matters: Kinodynamic Planning with a Terminal Cost and Learned Uncertainty in Belief State-Cost Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09046)
*Zhuoyun Zhong,Seyedali Golestaneh,Constantinos Chamzas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces KiTe, a terminal-cost formulation for kinodynamic planning that optimizes both trajectory cost and terminal-state quality (goal preference/reliability), with theoretical guarantees of asymptotic optimality and improved goal-reaching probability bounds under uncertainty, validated in simulation and real-world pushing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sampling-based kinodynamic planners optimize accumulated trajectory costs but treat goal reaching as a feasibility check rather than explicitly optimizing terminal-state quality such as goal preference or reliability under uncertainty.

Method: Introduces a terminal-cost formulation for kinodynamic planning (proven to preserve AO-RRT's asymptotic optimality), extends it to belief space with Wasserstein distance minimization (improving goal-reaching probability lower bound), and enables learning-based belief dynamics for systems without analytical uncertainty models.

Result: Experiments on Flappy Bird, Car Parking, and Planar Pushing show KiTe consistently improves goal-reaching success under uncertainty, and real-world Planar Pushing validates effectiveness with learned dynamics and uncertainty.

Conclusion: The terminal-cost formulation effectively optimizes terminal-state quality alongside trajectory cost, improving goal-reaching reliability under uncertainty, with theoretical guarantees and practical applicability using learned models.

Abstract: In many real-world robotic tasks, robots must generate dynamically feasible motions that reliably reach desired goals even under uncertainty. Yet existing sampling-based kinodynamic planners typically optimize accumulated trajectory costs and treat goal reaching as a feasibility check, rather than explicitly optimizing terminal-state quality, such as goal preference or goal-reaching reliability. In this work, we introduce a terminal-cost formulation for kinodynamic planning that allows terminal-state quality to be optimized alongside accumulated trajectory cost. We prove that AO-RRT, an asymptotically optimal kinodynamic planner, preserves its asymptotic optimality under this augmented objective. We further extend the formulation to belief space and prove that minimizing the Wasserstein distance between the terminal belief and the goal improves a lower bound on the probability of reaching the goal region. The resulting planner, KiTe, uses this terminal-cost objective to encode goal preferences and improve reliability under uncertainty. To support systems without analytical uncertainty models, we learn dynamics and process uncertainty directly from data and integrate the learned belief dynamics into planning. Experiments on Flappy Bird, Car Parking, and Planar Pushing show that KiTe consistently improves goal-reaching success under uncertainty. Real-world Planar Pushing experiments further demonstrate that KiTe can plan effectively with learned dynamics and uncertainty. Source code is available at https://github.com/elpis-lab/KiTe.

</details>


### [356] [Automated Robotic Moisture Monitoring in Agricultural Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09050)
*Senthil Palanisamy,Akila I. S*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robotic system using moisture sensors and Dijkstra's algorithm on aerial images to autonomously monitor soil moisture in large agricultural fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual moisture monitoring in large-scale plantations is tedious and inefficient.

Method: Field divided into grids with moisture sensors; dry sensors trigger a robot to navigate using Dijkstra's shortest path on aerial images; robot computes total moisture via image processing.

Result: A prototype was built with a small study field and overhead camera, demonstrating an automated moisture monitoring system.

Conclusion: An efficient and economical automated system for monitoring agricultural field moisture was successfully developed and tested.

Abstract: Monitoring moisture level of land in a large-scale plantation is tedious. The main objective of this project is to use a robotic kit in collaboration with the on-field moisture sensor circuits, thereby creating an efficient and economical moisture monitoring system. A large agriculture field is divided into smaller grids. Each grid is placed with a moisture sensor. Whenever a sensor reports the soil to be dry, the robot goes to the concerned field for inspection. The path to the concerned field is found by applying Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm on the aerial image of the field. Then the total moisture content of the field is calculated by the robot using suitable image processing algorithms and reported accordingly. For developing and testing this work, a small study field was set up above which a camera was mounted at an appropriate height to capture its aerial view. Thus a prototype for an automated system of monitoring agricultural fields' moisture has been developed through this work.

</details>


### [357] [Octopus Protocol: One-Shot Hardware Discovery and Control for AI Agents via Infrastructure-as-Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09055)
*Quilee Simeon,Justin M. Wei,Yile Fan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Octopus Protocol collapses hardware bring-up for agentic robotics to a single shell command by having a coding agent automatically probe, identify, interface, serve, and deploy MCP servers for connected devices in ~10-15 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The dominant engineering cost of bringing up new hardware for agent control is writing drivers/SDKs/primitives for target hardware, which existing systems presuppose already exist.

Method: A five-stage pipeline (PROBE, IDENTIFY, INTERFACE, SERVE, DEPLOY) where a coding agent discovers devices, infers capabilities, generates an MCP server with typed tools, and deploys it as an HTTP endpoint, with a persistent daemon for monitoring and healing.

Result: Validated on PC/WSL, Apple Silicon macOS, Raspberry Pi 4, and a commercial 6-DOF robotic arm. One command onboards hardware in ~10-15 minutes, exposing up to 30 MCP tools.

Conclusion: Two principles make this work: protocols are prompts (not code), and the coding agent is the runtime. MCP-compliant clients can perform closed-loop control through auto-generated tools.

Abstract: Recent agentic-robotics systems, from Code-asPolicies to modern vision-language-action (VLA) foundation models, presuppose that drivers, SDKs, or ROS-style primitives for the target hardware already exist. Writing those primitives is the dominant engineering cost of bringing up new hardware for agent control. We present Octopus Protocol, a system that collapses that cost to a single shell command. Given only raw OS access and a language-model API key, a coding agent executes a five-stage pipeline--PROBE, IDENTIFY, INTERFACE, SERVE, DEPLOY--to discover connected devices, infer their capabilities, generate a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server with typed tools, and deploy it as a live HTTP endpoint. A persistent daemon then monitors the system, heals broken code, and perceives physical state through the camera tools it generated for itself. Two architectural principles make this work: protocols are prompts, not code, and the coding agent is the runtime. We validate the system on three heterogeneous platforms (PC/WSL, Apple Silicon macOS, Raspberry Pi 4) and on a commercial 6-DOF robotic arm with USB camera feedback. One command onboards the hardware in ~10-15 minutes and exposes up to 30 MCP tools; an MCP-compliant client then performs closed-loop visual-motor control through tools no human wrote.

</details>


### [358] [Smoothing Out the Edges: Continuous-Time Estimation with Gaussian Process Motion Priors on Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09073)
*Connor Holmes,Sven Lilge,Zi Cong Guo,Frank Dellaert,Timothy D. Barfoot*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper simplifies GP continuous-time state estimation by reframing it using factor graphs, offering easier implementation and three working examples in GTSAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonparametric GP continuous-time estimation has seen less adoption despite its advantages; the authors aim to rectify this by providing a simpler explanation.

Method: The authors reinterpret GP continuous-time estimation through the lens of factor graphs and provide three working examples implemented in GTSAM.

Result: Three working examples implemented in GTSAM demonstrating the simplified GP estimation approach.

Conclusion: The factor graph explanation lowers the barrier to adopting GP-based continuous-time estimation in robotics.

Abstract: Continuous-time state estimation is gaining in popularity due to its abilities to provide smooth solutions, handle asynchronous sensors, and interpolate between data points. While there are two main paradigms, parametric (e.g., temporal basis functions, splines) and nonparametric (Gaussian processes), the latter has seen less adoption despite its technical advantages and relative ease of implementation. In this article, we seek to rectify this situation by providing a new simplified explanation of GP continuous-time estimation rooted in the language of factor graphs, which have become the de facto estimation paradigm in much of robotics. To simplify onboarding, we also provide three working examples implemented in the popular GTSAM estimation framework.

</details>


### [359] [HyDRA Scorpion: A Cost-effective and Modular ROV for Real-Time Underwater Inspection, Intervention, and Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09093)
*Anika Tabassum Orchi,Md Farhan Zaman,Md Darain Khan,Md Alamgir,Mahbubul Islam,Md. Jobayer Rahman,A. M. Zayed Abdullah,Md Mehrab Hossain Khan,Md. Kutub Al Baki,Iftekharul Islam,Shakil Ahmed,Md Sadique Hossain,Md Muzahidul Islam,Shah Mohammad Seaman,Nusrat Jahan Piyal,Shekh Md. Saifur Rahman,Fahim Hafiz,A. K. M. Muzahidul Islam,M. Rezwan Khan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HyDRA Scorpion is a low-cost ROV integrating AI-driven perception, 4-DoF maneuverability, dual manipulators, and pressure-tolerant housing, validated through pressure testing (4 bar), station-keeping (±0.15 m), underwater object detection (mAP 0.89), and tangle-free manipulator rotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High cost of commercial ROVs and a gap between mechanically capable platforms and integrated intelligence create a significant barrier to underwater science access.

Method: Developed a low-cost, locally manufacturable ROV with 4-DoF maneuverability, dual manipulators, custom pressure-tested housing, and an AI-driven perception stack for real-time object detection and 3D mapping.

Result: Leak-free operation at 4 bar pressure, stable station-keeping within ±0.15 m, underwater object detection mAP of 0.89 with real-time inference, and 360° tangle-free manipulator rotation.

Conclusion: The HyDRA Scorpion successfully demonstrates that an affordable, AI-integrated ROV platform can achieve robust performance comparable to or exceeding conventional systems, lowering the barrier to underwater science.

Abstract: A Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) is a tethered underwater robot used for tasks like inspection and intervention. While essential tools for underwater science, the high cost of commercial ROVs and a persistent gap between mechanically capable platforms and those with integrated intelligence create a significant barrier to access. HyDRA Scorpion differs from conventional systems by addressing these challenges, integrating an advanced, AI-driven perception stack with in-situ measurement capabilities onto a low-cost, locally manufacturable platform. The system combines 4-DoF maneuverability, dual manipulators, and a custom pressure-tested housing. Experimental results validate the system's robustness and performance. Leak-free operation was confirmed through prolonged pressure testing of the electronics housing to 4 bar, equivalent to the pressure of a 304.8-meter water depth approximately in a simulated environment, with no moisture ingress detected. The vehicle also demonstrated stable station-keeping, maintaining its position within a tight tolerance of $\(\pm\)0.15$ meters under external disturbances. The onboard AI module achieved underwater object detection mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.89 with real-time inference, length and 3D-mapping based distance measurement. Also, 4-DoF manipulator arm can grip and maintain dual-function manipulator feature which support 360 degree tangle-free rotation.

</details>


### [360] [IMPACT: An Implicit Active-Set Augmented Lagrangian for Fast Contact-Implicit Trajectory Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09127)
*Jiayun Li,Dejian Gong,Georgia Chalvatzaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes IMPACT, an augmented-Lagrangian method for contact-implicit trajectory optimization that achieves 2.9x-70x speedups (mean 13.8x) over strong baselines by identifying contact modes on the fly, with hardware validation on a pushing task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MPCC-based CITO methods remain numerically ill-conditioned and lack systematic, scalable solution strategies, limiting broader applicability.

Method: An augmented-Lagrangian approach to CITO that identifies implicit contact-mode branches on the fly during trajectory optimization iterations (IMPACT), with an efficient C++ implementation.

Result: 2.9x-70x speedups on CITO benchmarks (geometric mean 13.8x), improved control quality on CI-MPC dexterous manipulation tasks, and hardware demonstration on a T-shaped object pushing task.

Conclusion: The proposed IMPACT method provides a more efficient and principled solver for contact-implicit trajectory optimization with stationarity guarantees and practical hardware applicability.

Abstract: Contact-implicit trajectory optimization (CITO) has attracted growing attention as a unified framework for planning and control in contact-rich robotic tasks. Recent approaches have demonstrated promising results in manipulation and locomotion without requiring a prescribed contact-mode schedule. It is well known that the underlying mathematical programs with complementarity constraints (MPCCs) remain numerically ill-conditioned, and systematic, scalable solution strategies for CITO remain an active area of research. More efficient and principled solvers that can handle contact constraints are therefore essential to broaden the applicability of CITO. In this work, we develop an augmented-Lagrangian approach to CITO for solving MPCC-based CITO with stationarity guarantees. The method can be interpreted as identifying the implicit contact-mode branches on the fly during the trajectory optimization (TO) iterations; we call this approach IMPACT (IMPlicit contact ACtive-set Trajectory optimization). We provide an efficient C++ implementation tailored to trajectory-optimization workloads and evaluate it on the open-source CITO and contact-implicit model predictive control (CI-MPC) benchmarks. On CITO, IMPACT achieves 2.9x-70x speedups over strong baselines (geometric mean 13.8x). On CI-MPC, we show improved control quality for contact-rich trajectories on dexterous manipulation tasks in simulation. Finally, we demonstrate the proposed method on real robotic hardware on a T-shaped object pushing task.

</details>


### [361] [Beyond Self-Play: Hierarchical Reasoning for Continuous Motion in Closed-Loop Traffic Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09153)
*Weifan Zhang,Xiaofeng Zhao,Adel Bazzi,Mingrui Li,Yifan Wei,Dengfeng Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical framework combining Stackelberg-style multi-agent reinforcement learning for high-level intention reasoning with low-level continuous motion control, plus a hybrid co-training scheme, to achieve more socially aware and realistic closed-loop traffic simulation than self-play approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-play RL approaches for traffic simulation are scalable but fail to capture socially aware behaviors of real human drivers, as their equilibrium strategies lack realistic interaction reasoning.

Method: A hierarchical architecture: (1) high-level Stackelberg-style MARL module generates interaction-aware intention commands; (2) low-level continuous motion module translates commands into physically consistent control sequences; (3) hybrid co-training scheme combining MARL with auxiliary recovery supervision to mitigate distribution shift.

Result: The framework achieves superior control smoothness and safety compared to self-play and passive imitation baselines, while maintaining competitive traffic efficiency in SUMO-based urban network experiments.

Conclusion: Combining hierarchical Stackelberg MARL reasoning with low-level motion control and hybrid co-training can produce more socially realistic and safer traffic agents at scale.

Abstract: Closed-loop traffic simulation requires agents that are both scalable and behaviorally realistic. Recent self-play reinforcement learning approaches demonstrate strong scalability, but their equilibrium strategies fail to capture the socially aware behaviors of real human drivers. We propose a hierarchical architecture that goes beyond self-play by combining high-level multi-agent interaction reasoning with low-level continuous trajectory realization. Specifically, a Stackelberg-style Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) module generates interaction-aware intention commands. These commands condition a low-level continuous motion module, translating the strategic intent into physically consistent, scene-responsive control sequences. To mitigate distribution shift in closed-loop deployment, we introduce a hybrid co-training scheme combining MARL with auxiliary recovery supervision. Experiments on a SUMO-based urban network demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior control smoothness and safety compared to self-play and passive imitation baselines, while maintaining competitive traffic efficiency.

</details>


### [362] [SHIELD: Scalable Optimal Control with Certification using Duality and Convexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09171)
*Hansung Kim,Siddharth H. Nair,Francesco Borrelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SHIELD is a hierarchical algorithm that reduces decision-variable dimension and constraints in ℓ1-regularized convex programs using strong convexity and Lagrangian duality to safely discard constraints and variables, accelerated by a transformer-based neural network, achieving order-of-magnitude speedups in stochastic model predictive control for complex traffic scenarios while preserving feasibility and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve computational speedups in ℓ1-regularized convex programs (specifically stochastic MPC) while maintaining certifiable feasibility and closed-loop safety in complex, multi-modal traffic scenarios.

Method: A hierarchical algorithm using strong convexity and Lagrangian duality to derive certificates for safely discarding constraints (all removed constraints remain satisfied) and decision variables (all removed variables are null), plus a transformer-based deep neural network to accelerate dual certificate inference.

Result: Order-of-magnitude computational speedups over full-dimensional SMPC while preserving feasibility and closed-loop safety in complex, multi-modal traffic scenarios.

Conclusion: SHIELD demonstrates the practicality of certifiably safe, lightweight MPC in complex driving scenes, achieving significant speedups without sacrificing safety or feasibility.

Abstract: We present SHIELD, a hierarchical algorithm that reduces both the decision-variable dimension and the constraint set in $\ell_1$-regularized convex programs. From strong convexity and Lagrangian duality, we derive certificates that \emph{safely} discard constraints and decision variables while guaranteeing that all removed constraints remain satisfied and all removed variables are null. To further accelerate the proposed algorithm, we propose a transformer-based deep neural network to guide the dual certificate inference. We validate SHIELD on stochastic model predictive control (SMPC) in complex, multi-modal traffic scenarios, comparing against a full-dimensional SMPC policy. Numerical simulations demonstrate order-of-magnitude computational speedups while preserving feasibility and closed-loop safety, highlighting the practicality of certifiably safe, lightweight MPC in complex driving scenes.

</details>


### [363] [Continuum Robot Modeling with Action Conditioned Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09216)
*Jiong Lin,Jinchen Ruan,Hod Lipson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a data-driven self-modeling framework using point cloud flow matching to predict steady-state 3D shapes of tendon-driven continuum robots (TDCRs) from motor actuation states, demonstrating improved accuracy over prior methods in both simulation and real hardware experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting the shape of TDCRs at steady state is challenging due to continuous deformation, complex tendon routing, compliance, friction, and fabrication variability.

Method: A lightweight 3D printed TDCR hardware with RGB-D data collection, and a point cloud flow matching model mapping motor actuation states to settled 3D geometry, trained on randomly sampled quasi-static configurations.

Result: Improved shape prediction accuracy under CD and EMD metrics on simulated 2-/3-/5-module TDCRs and real 2-/3-module robots; also generalizes to tip payload conditioning in simulation.

Conclusion: A data-driven self-modeling framework effectively predicts quasi-static TDCR geometry, with potential generalization to payload-conditioned shape prediction.

Abstract: Predicting the shape of tendon driven continuum robots (TDCRs) at steady state from actuation remains challenging due to continuous deformation, complex tendon routing, compliance, friction, and fabrication variability. In this paper, we address this problem as kinematic self modeling conditioned on action.
